body:
@meganewday just visit this page, that's enough. https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/getting-started/download/

`CDN` via jsDelivr

```html
<link href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.3.2/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css" rel="stylesheet" integrity="sha384-T3c6CoIi6uLrA9TneNEoa7RxnatzjcDSCmG1MXxSR1GAsXEV/Dwwykc2MPK8M2HN" crossorigin="anonymous">
<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.3.2/dist/js/bootstrap.bundle.min.js" integrity="sha384-C6RzsynM9kWDrMNeT87bh95OGNyZPhcTNXj1NW7RuBCsyN/o0jlpcV8Qyq46cDfL" crossorigin="anonymous"></script>
```

`NPM`

```js
npm install bootstrap@5.3.2
```

`Yarn`

```js
yarn add bootstrap@5.3.2
```

`Composer`

```js
composer require twbs/bootstrap:5.3.2
```

body:
The option `pause` determines the behavior when a hovering over the carousel. If set to `false`, hovering over the carousel won't pause it.

If you don't want the carousel to cycle automatically, you need to use the option `interval` set to `false`.
I guess you can switch back to a number value when the carousel is ready to cycle (I haven't tried it myself).

https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.1/components/carousel/#options

author: mdo

body:
> they're not required to make the dropdown etc work correctly, if that was the question @thewebartisan7 ... and even for accessibility, they're more a "nice to have" than a hard requirement.

If it's optional, but "nice to have" that means it's _better_ to have it, right? So removing it in #36487 is therefore somewhat _worse_? As accessibility is a legal requirement for many of us, it would be nice if the examples reflected the accessible version. Maybe by "question of taste" you mean that it doesn't necessarily improve or reduce accessibility either way, however, I don't see anywhere in the changes an explanation (such as an example) that provides guidance. Based on the code changes, it seems like the recommendation is to remove these two attributes which are "confusing", though it's unclear to me if it's confusing for users of AT (which would be very bad, and it makes sense to fix the problem) or if it's confusing to developers (like me, who might end up at this discussion looking for an explanation).

I hope this helps clarify what this all looks like to users like me who are not in the loop. Thank you all for your amazing efforts to build the tools that make everything better.

author: ibrahimAboelsuod

body:
This is how I did it in Angular@16, with SASS and RTLCSS in package.json, before building the application:
1. build Bootstrap SCSS (with your customized variables), with sass, as /assets/bootstrap.min.css
2. build the RTL file with rtlcss, from /assets/bootstrap.min.css to /assets/bootstrap.rtl.min.css
3. in your index.html. link the /assets/bootstrap.rtl.min.css file, and set dir="rtl" and lang="ar" in the HTML element

So, my build script in package.json looks something like:
"build-all": "sass ... bootstrap.scss ./assets/bootstrap.min.css & rtlcss ... ./assets/bootstrap.min.css ./assets/bootstrap.rtl.min.css & ng build ...",
(the script paths depend on the bootstrap files location, and root options in angular.json, but you get the idea)

If you need to switch between LTR and RTL without reloading the page, you can update the DOM to change the link SRC and the dir/lang attributes
author: Filini

body:
As per the [Bootstrap docs](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/getting-started/rtl/#get-familiar), you need a tool ([`rtlcss`](https://rtlcss.com/)) to process the generated css file from your scss project. Then `rtlcss`, in turn, generates an RTL css file.

1. Step One: compile sass to css (eg. `style.css`)
2. Step two: run `rtlcss` on `style.css` and generate `style.rtl.css`
3. Step three: manually change the stylesheet file on the document using javascript based on event

author: elchininet

body:
Some bootstrap classes are still undercooked when it comes to `rtl`. 

i use `mx` tag instead of `ms` or `me` when it comes to multi language sites (that has both `ltr` and `rtl`). To a degree, it gives symmetry.

And yes default `ms` and `me` seems misleading when doing website in `rtl` format.

for now i use custom css like `[dir="rtl"] .ms-<X> {margin-right: <valueBasedonX>}`. Extra work but helps in avoiding confusion.

author: mdo

body:
Our implementation currently puts component-specific variables on the component level as to not flood the `:root` element. For example, if you're customizing your build by not importing half our components, we don't want those to still be there or require additional enabling/disabling on a per-component basis.

You will notice though in the `_root.scss` stylesheet that we have a growing number of global CSS variables that we reuse across the project for shared styles. Things like colors, fonts, borders, and more. Those are variables that can be overridden on the `:root` or `<body>` and have their changes globally inherited. This set of root variables will continue to grow with dark mode support coming in #35857 where we add additional, more nuanced shared colors (kind of like Apple's HIG).

With v5 at least, one of the main goals of adding CSS variables is to enable color mode support. But, it's not a feasible solution to have to customize every single component's colors to make that happen. As such, we've opted for fewer global variables and more component variables. This isn't a perfect setup yet—mixing Sass functionality with CSS variables is a little messy. However, this will continue to be iterated on and refined heading into a v6 eventually.
author: mdo

body:
Thanks for the explanation, @mdo. Not flooding `:root` with component-specific variables if those components aren’t being compiled into the final stylesheet makes sense.

Love the work that’s been done on this front so far, though. And keen to see where it heads in the future. Kudos to every one who’s been involved!

body: `.form-group` was dropped in v5 because it was only a `margin-bottom` declaration. It didn't make much sense to have `.mb-3` and `.form-group` doing the same thing, so we went with the former utility class. It's also worth clarifying for other readers that `.row`s haven't had any vertical spacing by default in v4 or v5. This is strictly about `.form-group` no longer existing in v5.

body:
I found this excellent tutorial, which uses using `display : grid` instead of `display : flex` to center. (https://chrisbracco.com/css-truly-center-a-single-child-element-horizontally-when-siblings-are-present/). The author explains the central issue, "The “centered” child element is only horizontally centered within the remaining space in the parent element, and not the “true center” of the parent element. In this case the width of the centered element is equal to the width of the parent element minus any sibling element(s). The centered element can only be “truly centered” if the width of child elements on both the left and right of it are equal." The tutorial solution boils down to this: ```
Center
``` The "solution" applied to my nav looks like the following below. Hopefully overriding `display: flex` in container-fluid, or any other navbar class I've used, with `display: grid` won't cause any issues. I haven't found any yet. BTW, I recommend that Boostrap provide a way to truly centring navbar components without the user having to add custom CSS. Please let me know if there is an easy way to do this with the current Bootstrap classes. I added the `truly-center-grid` to the container-fluid div. I didn't bother adding truly-center-child to the name-brand `` link or the navbar-nav `
    ` for the menu since specifying `grid-template-columns: 1fr auto 1fr` in the truly-center-grid class already places these components accordingly. At a later date, if I want a right-align search box, I can just add the bootstrap "text-end" class to the search form element/div. CSS ``` .truly-center-grid { display: grid !important; grid-template-columns: 1fr auto 1fr; align-items: center; } ``` HTML ```
    Samba du Soleil
    • Home
    • Band
    • Music
    • Events
    • More
      • Scores
      • Contact
      • Something else here
    ```

author: mdo

body:
Hee there,

I've been using css grids for quite some time to build websites (creating fallbacks by first building the element using display block combined with floating, or just using display flex and putting the css grid code in the `@support(display:grid){}` feature query). A lot of browsers started to support css grids from 2017 onwards. Now that global support is 94.94% (!), and with Internet Explorer officially dead, I think it's time to go forward and implement css grids for Bootstrap as soon as possible. What are we waiting for?

And why the opt-in for css grids? If you don't want to use css grids, you don't put the d-grid class in your html, right?

For context, and to be clear, in Bootstrap you can use layouts in 3 ways:
1. Bootstrap grid system (container-row-col)
2. Flexbox
3. CSS Grid (opt-in)

An even more radical idea: I would propose to get rid of the Bootstrap grid system entirely, from let's say Bootstrap 6, and replace the container-row-col structure with a new Bootstrap grid system based on css grids, which does this in a cleaner way.

I find the Bootstrap grid system hacky because of the use of negative margins to create the column structure. When applying margins to columns, the negative margins (of these columns) is interfered with and the layout doesn't look like what you would expect. The same goes for applying padding to containers, which already have a default padding set to them.

Concerning the choice of flexbox vs css grids, I would always keep using both, for flexbox in my view, combines perfectly well together with css grids. It's a match made in heaven for us layout perfectionists.

To see the main discussion about implementing css grids, see #31813

body:
This is covered a bit in our docs: https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.2/customize/sass/#importing. If you include the whole of Bootstrap, you can place your variables first, but you may have issues with variable and map re-use later in our codebase. In addition, functions for things like SVGs and colors and more won't be available.

In contrast to importing individual pieces, you get full control and access to the functions. There are a few code comments that talk about this in the Customize> Sass page.

> // Include any default variable overrides here (though functions won't be available)

> 1. Include functions first (so you can manipulate colors, SVGs, calc, etc)

body:
@antonyspericorn If you're just wanting the modal to appear on page load without the use of JavaScript/jQuery/etc., then it's more than just `show` and `d-block` that you need to have on the modal. Make sure you also have `aria-hidden="false"`, since the default is set to `true`. Also, make sure to remove the default `fade` class from your modal. This is likely causing your console errors. Example: ```html
Modal title
...
``` source: [v5.1 #live-demo](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.1/components/modal/#live-demo) Side Note: If the modal is always open, then consider not using a modal component, but rather a [card](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.1/components/card/#header-and-footer) component. The modal component being always open might have a fixed position on your screen (unless that's your intention). The card component can use header/footer like a modal. Example using card component styled like modal: ```html
Card title
Not a Modal

Styled like a modal, but actually a card

``` source: [v5.1 card](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.1/components/card/#header-and-footer) screenshot of example:

body:
Include the font however you like (Google Fonts has many options listed on their site), and then update the `font-family` wherever you need it. Usually it's enough to override that on the `<body>` element.

For example, add this to your HTML inside the `<head>`:

```html
<link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com"> 
<link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.gstatic.com" crossorigin> 
<link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Roboto&display=swap" rel="stylesheet">
```

And in your CSS:

```css
body {
  font-family: 'Roboto', sans-serif;
}
```

body:
@qoietsammy it looks like you're using some templating engine (aspx?) to allow conditionals within your HTML. `<% if(campground.images.length > 1) %{>` When using any sort of templating engine (such as Jinja), something to bear in mind is that any component that uses an `id=""` attribute (in your case: `id="carouselExampleControls"`) must always be unique. IDs on an HTML page must always be unique, otherwise they won't work properly. It's quite possible that since you've got more than 1 carousel item to display, they're all "stacking" perhaps? Would you be able to provide a bit more context and code if that doesn't help? Tip: something I like to do when in any forloops with a templating engine to make my IDs unique is to incorporate the forloop counter into the ID. Example from Flask/Django (Jinja) syntax: ```jinja {% for foo in foobar %}
....
{% endfor %} ```

I've never used EJS, but it seems to work quite similar to any of the others like Jinja.

I tested your code in two places; [codepen.io](https://codepen.io/) and also an [EJS templates playground](https://table.listws.com/table/canvas/content) that I found.

Interestingly enough, on codepen, the carousel behaved as I would expect it to.
However, when I test it on the EJS playground, I'm experiencing the same as you with the Previous/Next buttons having the white opacity.

Upon investigating further in the DOM, I found one strange difference.
The Bootstrap file for `_carousel.scss` line [#104](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/main/scss/_carousel.scss#L104) contains:

```css
background: none;
```

This doesn't seem to be the case on the EJS playground though for some reason.
Something you can try to test,... manually adjust it on your own CSS file perhaps?

```css
.carousel-control-next, .carousel-control-prev {
    background: none;
}
```

body:
@arcanisgk 
You would need to use something like [jsPDF](https://github.com/MrRio/jsPDF) to convert the page into a PDF.

However, please be aware that just by itself, it cannot retain *[Bootstrap]* styles and formatting. You would need to include a plugin like [html2canvas](https://github.com/niklasvh/html2canvas).

If you don't need to dynamically generate the PDF files, another simple option I've used before is the Chrome Developer Tools.

- PC: Press **Ctrl + Shift + I**, then **Ctrl + Shift P**.
- Mac: **Press Command + Option + I**, then **Command + Shift P**.
- Then type **"screenshot"** to see the four screenshot options.

From there, you can simply save the screenshot locally as a PDF file on your system.

Hope this is helpful in some way for your task!

body:
@bubblypatrick 
Not entirely certain here, but something to consider is the fact that Bootstrap version 5 completely changes syntax from previous versions.

The newer version (5+) adds `-bs-` into the component data attributes, and it will not work with older versions. I see this daily with some junior developers that I mentor... Incorporating older CDN links, but grabbing version 5 components.

Perhaps food for thought on your issue, could have something to do with it...?

Example:

Version 3.4.x Modal
`data-toggle="modal"`

Version 4.6.x Modal
`data-toggle="modal"`

Version 5.2.x Modal
`data-bs-toggle="modal"`

Note: the `-bs-` (bootstrap) identifier in between.

Do we need to change the position of this map in our `custom.scss` file so that it only modifies some colors but keeps the others (like **danger**) intact?

author: gerardnico

body:
Yeah, but i mean, it doesn't completely solve the issue. There is still unused css moving around, regardless of whether or not it is being cached. I believe there should be a better way to deliver only requested css elements without the need to cache unwanted data. To do so, I think the second solution that i have already posted above somehow addressed the issue, but it would introduce a lot of `<link>` tag within the head element. Moreover, I am also thinking of building a cli that can accomplish this task. For instance, say you want to use the `btn` element; with such tool, namely `bootstrap-cli`, you can fetch the desired css elements like:

```sh
bootstrap-cli pull btn
``` 

Running the above command will fetch all the styling related to the `btn` and store it within a `style.css` file. This way, you are only using the required css elements, which results in much more efficient css file.

Regardless of which solution to use to solve this problem, i believe that having a large css file stored somewhere is a bit problematic, which results in lots of unwanted css declarations.

What do you think?

body:
Here's a little rundown of what's coming:

- Yes, there will be a patch release for v5.2.1. Might try to do this remotely (for me) this week, otherwise it'll be next week.
- v5.3.0 is the next minor release, the focus of which is dark mode (see #35857)
- v5.4.0 may come and could see some new JS components added (@GeoSot has some demos going). If not, this could go into v6, but depends on the team's bandwidth.
- Then likely onto v6.0.0 with some dependency updates, browser support changes, theming improvements, layout improvements, Sass modules (most likely), and other modern/future frontend improvements.

author: Fatbat

body: I am also experiencing the same - scrollspy is essentially unfunctional for a long single page website. The old behavior (bs4) would trigger scrollspy when the top of the targeted element crossed the top of the bounding element, and you could change the vertical offset. This worked perfectly for a long single page website. Now with intersection observer, the new behavior triggets scrollspy when a portion of the targeted element is visible. This isn't exact at all. Even Bootstrap docs examples are sloppy and imprecise. Intersection observer is great for some things but for scrollspy it's a nightmare!

body:
There are 2 key points you may want to understand:

1. Use a wrapper `<div>` to style you menu content. (Inside `.container-fluid` in my example). Let Bootstrap does its job for `<navbar>` and `<container-fluid>`. You manage your own styles using wrappers.
2. Use Bootstrap's Toggler system for navbar. Please see [Responsive behaviors - Navbar - Bootstrap Docs](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.2/components/navbar/#responsive-behaviors). It contains a `<button>` as toggler and a `<div>` as a list of menu. Please make sure you use a good "id" to toggler only one element.

I created a demo for you: https://codepen.io/Edditoria/pen/NWMpmdJ

And, I added a `<div.navbar-toggler-wrapper>` for better style for the hamburger menu. It is not necessary but only "not going to hurt your eyes". 😸 

---

Bootstrap is highly rely on breakpoints for screen (media queries), rather than contents (element queries). And make sure it display properly on modern browsers, without or with-less Javascript.

For your case, you will need Javascript to get the width of the whole navbar. It can only be done **after** the browser renders the page (that users may use vary fonts, scales and other settings). I think Bootstrap does not have this feature.

Thanks.

body:
Consume the files in https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/tree/main/js/dist ?

Or, in your JS code, `import { Collapse } from 'bootstrap/dist/js/bootstrap/bootstrap.esm.js';`?

As told in Bootstrap [docs][docs], it is quite complicated if you do it yourself. And, since you are developing website, I would use a bundler to resolve NPM packages, tree-shaking and distribute JS files for browser compatibility , e.g. Rollup, Webpack. Example:

```js
import { Collapse } from 'bootstrap'; // Type of Collapse is a class.
// You code...

// Or simply
export default Collapse
```

[docs]: https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.2/getting-started/javascript/#using-bootstrap-as-a-module

body:
Knowing that AdminLTE provides a nice tested solution with plenty of component choices, and 'plain' BS provides a solid base and some standard components, it comes the moment that you, and only you, can take this decision, consider it in combination of your UI needs and the tools you use.

I was on the same thoughts some years ago, but in my case I was using a php template engine that was supporting php-components usage (easy and in one place, maintained blocks & markup), and the frontend wasn't too demanding.  At that time, using webpack lazy loading for my assets, avoiding keeping a js-vendor folder full of scripts, and use only one or two script tags in my code, seemed probably a better solution for me.  

3 years later, I haven't regretted my decision, as I kept the dependencies to the minimum, but yes, there have been times during js refactoring and UI addons, where I needed to implement some minor stuff myself

body:
hey! It's my understanding that the only reason that the Bootstrap v4 docs are referencing and using jQuery 3.5.1 (and not the latest 3.6.x) is because IE10 tests were failing.

See https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/33243 for details.

As IE10 usage is miniscule and has been deprecated by Microsoft for a long time, you should be completely fine to use jQuery 3.6.1 with Bootstrap 4.6.2 with no issues at all.

body:
To change the background behind a title, create a custom.css file and add:

```css
.header-title {
  background-color: #desiredColor; /* Replace with your color */
}
```

Include it in your HTML after Bootstrap's CSS:

```html
<link rel="stylesheet" href="path/to/bootstrap.css">
<link rel="stylesheet" href="path/to/custom.css">
```

author: patrickhlauke

body:
My 2 cents. Few elements that could help figuring out what to do next.

### Option 1: Update `.browserlistrc`

<details>
  <summary>Current browser support</summary>
npx autoprefixer --info                                                                                                           (0.000001 hrs)

Browsers:
  Chrome for Android: 105
  Firefox for Android: 104
  QQ Browser: 13.1
  UC for Android: 13.4
  Android: 105
  Chrome: 106, 105, 104, 103, 102, 101, 100, 99, 98, 97, 96, 95, 94, 93, 92, 91, 90, 89, 88, 87, 86, 85, 84, 83, 81, 80, 79, 78, 77, 76, 75, 74, 73, 72, 71, 70, 69, 68, 67, 66, 65, 64, 63, 62, 61, 60
  Edge: 105, 104, 103
  Firefox: 105, 104, 103, 102, 101, 100, 99, 98, 97, 96, 95, 94, 93, 92, 91, 90, 89, 88, 87, 86, 85, 84, 83, 82, 81, 80, 79, 78, 77, 76, 75, 74, 73, 72, 71, 70, 69, 68, 67, 66, 65, 64, 63, 62, 61, 60
  iOS Safari: 16.0, 15.6, 15.5, 15.4, 15.2-15.3, 15.0-15.1, 14.5-14.8, 14.0-14.4, 13.4-13.7, 13.3, 13.2, 13.0-13.1, 12.2-12.5, 12.0-12.1
  KaiOS Browser: 2.5
  Opera Mini: all
  Opera Mobile: 64
  Opera: 91, 90, 89
  Safari: 16.0, 15.6, 15.5, 15.4, 15.2-15.3, 15.1, 15, 14.1, 14, 13.1, 13, 12.1, 12
  Samsung Internet: 18.0, 17.0

These browsers account for 95.97% of all users globally
</details>

By updating to `Chrome >= 86` + `Firefox >= 85` (and letting Firefox ESR) we got

<details>
  <summary>Updated .browserlistrc</summary>
Browsers:
  Chrome for Android: 105
  Firefox for Android: 104
  QQ Browser: 13.1
  UC for Android: 13.4
  Android: 105
  Chrome: 106, 105, 104, 103, 102, 101, 100, 99, 98, 97, 96, 95, 94, 93, 92, 91, 90, 89, 88, 87, 86
  Edge: 105, 104, 103
  Firefox: 105, 104, 103, 102, 101, 100, 99, 98, 97, 96, 95, 94, 93, 92, 91, 90, 89, 88, 87, 86, 85
  iOS Safari: 16.0, 15.6, 15.5, 15.4, 15.2-15.3, 15.0-15.1, 14.5-14.8, 14.0-14.4, 13.4-13.7, 13.3, 13.2, 13.0-13.1, 12.2-12.5, 12.0-12.1
  KaiOS Browser: 2.5
  Opera Mini: all
  Opera Mobile: 64
  Opera: 91, 90, 89
  Safari: 16.0, 15.6, 15.5, 15.4, 15.2-15.3, 15.1, 15, 14.1, 14, 13.1, 13, 12.1, 12
  Samsung Internet: 18.0, 17.0

These browsers account for 94.91% of all users globally
</details>

So we'd loose 1.06% of all users globally.
I haven't done the exercise but maybe some other browsers versions could need an update in this file as well (it would increase this number obviously).
I don't have the answer but I'm raising the question: would it be "OK"?

### If we update the file, when do we do it?

IMO updating this file shouldn't be in a dot version of Bootstrap.
Correct me if I'm wrong but we plan to use `prefers-color-scheme` for the dark mode support in v5.3.0 that seems to need at least Firefox 67 and Chrome 76 (source: https://caniuse.com/?search=prefers-color-scheme). So we'd need here an update of this `.browserlistrc` as well.

Extra-question here. If we don't update the file right now, do we need to rollback the modifications already merged for `:focus-visible`? (might be not so simple)

### Option 2: `focus-visible` polyfill

If we consider that this `.browserlistrc` should remain the same for now, another option is to use the `focus-visible` polyfill: https://github.com/WICG/focus-visible (that's what we use in Boosted actually). The drawbacks for this solution are the following:
* this polyfill is always enabled even if the user's browser support `:focus-visible`
* the syntax is a bit different than `:focus-visible` (see https://github.com/WICG/focus-visible#2-update-your-css)

body:
You're using an old version of `bootstrap.css` (v4) with an example from the actual documentation in v5:
```css
<!-- Bootstrap core CSS -->
    <link
      rel="stylesheet"
      href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@4.3.1/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css"
      integrity="sha384-ggOyR0iXCbMQv3Xipma34MD+dH/1fQ784/j6cY/iJTQUOhcWr7x9JvoRxT2MZw1T"
      crossorigin="anonymous"
    />
```

Replace it by `<link href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.2.2/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css" rel="stylesheet" integrity="sha384-Zenh87qX5JnK2Jl0vWa8Ck2rdkQ2Bzep5IDxbcnCeuOxjzrPF/et3URy9Bv1WTRi" crossorigin="anonymous">` and you should be good to go.

author: AjayTheWizard

body:
OK so you have this rule in __reboot.scss_: `text-align: var(--#{$prefix}body-text-align);` (`$prefix` is set to `bs-` in the project).
This rule is in `body {`.
So, in your browser (or in a CSS file in your project or with inline CSS with `<body style="--bs-body-text-align: end;">` for example) you can do stuff like that `--bs-body-text-align: end;` for example and the body text will be aligned on the right in LTR mode.

Our doc is obviously using Bootstrap so here is the rendering when I change this value dynamically in the browser:

![Screenshot 2022-10-22 at 10 24 48](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17381666/197328991-0a5ca663-b459-4fa0-a76a-c58f65b7daf2.png)

More info in [Content > Reboot > CSS variables](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.2/content/reboot/#css-variables)

body:
In your `<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap/dist/js/bootstrap.bundle.min.js" integrity="sha384-MrcW6ZMFYlzcLA8Nl+NtUVF0sA7MsXsP1UyJoMp4YLEuNSfAP+JcXn/tWtIaxVXM" crossorigin="anonymous"></script>` you probably kept the previous value of integrity.

It generates a log in the Dev Tools: `None of the "sha384" hashes in the integrity attribute match the content of the subresource.`

Replace by `<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap/dist/js/bootstrap.bundle.min.js" integrity="sha384-kenU1KFdBIe4zVF0s0G1M5b4hcpxyD9F7jL+jjXkk+Q2h455rYXK/7HAuoJl+0I4" crossorigin="anonymous"></script>` and it works

My advice would be to use rather ` <script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.2.3/dist/js/bootstrap.bundle.min.js" integrity="sha384-kenU1KFdBIe4zVF0s0G1M5b4hcpxyD9F7jL+jjXkk+Q2h455rYXK/7HAuoJl+0I4" crossorigin="anonymous"></script>` where the specific version is mentioned otherwise you'll have the same issue when we will release another version of Bootstrap.

body:
The ```<aside>``` element is used to wrap content that is indirectly related to the main content of the page. In the case of the Bootstrap 5 documentation, the left sidebar menu is directly related to the main content of the page, as it is the main navigation of the documentation. Therefore, using the ```<aside>``` element to wrap the left sidebar menu is not semantically correct.

Instead, it would be better to use a different HTML element, such as a ```<nav>``` element, to wrap the left sidebar menu. The ```<nav>``` element is specifically designed for navigation, and using it would be more semantically correct in this case.

body:
You can use the .collapse and .show classes to show different content for the offcanvas show menu and the expanded menu list. To do this, you can add the .collapse class to the offcanvas menu element, and then add the .show class to the expanded menu list element. This will hide the offcanvas menu by default, and show the expanded menu list instead. Then, when the offcanvas menu is toggled, the .collapse and .show classes will be toggled on the corresponding elements, which will show the offcanvas menu and hide the expanded menu list. ```
  • Offcanvas menu item 1
  • Offcanvas menu item 2
  • Offcanvas menu item 3
  • Expanded menu item 1
  • Expanded menu item 2
  • Expanded menu item 3
``` In this example, we have added the .collapse class to the offcanvas menu element, and the .show class to the expanded menu list element. This will hide the offcanvas menu and show the expanded menu list by default. When the offcanvas menu is toggled, the .collapse and .show classes will be toggled on the corresponding elements, which will show the offcanvas menu and hide

author: roywielens

body:
Hey @roywielens!

> I made my footer sticky, but some content is still showing below my footer.

Your footer is defined like this: `<footer class="footer fixed-bottom d-flex flex-wrap justify-content-between align-items-center py-3 my-4 border-top">`. There is a `.my-4` class for this `<footer>` so you have a top margin but also a bottom margin; this bottom margin is what displays the "empty space" below the footer. Try to just remove this `.my-4`.

While I was trying to find a solution for your example, I've noticed that it loads a lot of JS and CSS files mixing different versions of Bootstrap. You can probably only load the following:

```html
<!-- CSS only -->
<link href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.2.3/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css" rel="stylesheet" integrity="sha384-rbsA2VBKQhggwzxH7pPCaAqO46MgnOM80zW1RWuH61DGLwZJEdK2Kadq2F9CUG65" crossorigin="anonymous">
```

```html
<!-- JavaScript Bundle with Popper -->
<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.2.3/dist/js/bootstrap.bundle.min.js" integrity="sha384-kenU1KFdBIe4zVF0s0G1M5b4hcpxyD9F7jL+jjXkk+Q2h455rYXK/7HAuoJl+0I4" crossorigin="anonymous"></script>
```

I can't guarantee that it will work in all your website but based on your source code here's a CodePen to show you this simplification for loading your resources: https://codepen.io/julien-deramond/pen/PoaLQWR.

author: ayberkyasa

body:
A part of the answer (which is only my own interpretation) is that for commits merged into the main branch, it is safer to let the workflow do its work to avoid any regression. Moreover, our CI has special rules depending on the context (_main_ branch, PRs, etc.). That's why probably you'll find nothing by checking the history of, let's say, the _main_ branch.
In PRs, IMO it's because nobody thinks about it even if it would be better in some cases in terms of eco-design.

This is correct behavior right now, but we could change it. Changing "white" to black or similar doesn't make sense. We recommend using `bg-body` instead of `bg-white` for that one.

For `bg-light`, use `bg-body-secondary` or `bg-body-tertiary`. These new utilities and variables are designed to respect color modes better.

author: Alex-Toucan

body:
Sorry I lead you to the wrong path because I was not reading the error correctly. The error comes from `btnToActive.querySelector('svg use')`. In your website, you don't use the same structure as in our doc. We use a SVG (`<svg>`), and you're using an icon (`<i>`). So the query selector will return null and break the execution of the script. So you'll need to adapt our script if you want it to work in your case with these icons.

For a first test, I'd say you can remove the two calls to `showActiveTheme()`. You'll probably have a working theme switch but the active theme won't be shown properly in your dropdown.

author: denizgolbas

body:
[The current way RTL Bootstrap is built relies on RTLCSS](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.0/getting-started/rtl/#approach) and is meant to ease the switch: you can use the exact same markup (except the `lang` and `dir` attributes, obviously) and only switch from `bootstrap.min.css` to `bootstrap.rtl.min.css` for RTL languages.

When using the snippet to use both directions at the same time, you need to build you CSS by yourself:
1. use the provided snippet, 
2. then compile with Sass to get the `.ltr` wrapped file (expanded Sass styles to keep RTLCSS comments),
3. and finally run [RTLCSS](https://rtlcss.com/) on the outputted file to get the `.rtl` wrapped file.

You should get two different files, that you may merge and minify using Sass or anything else.

Then you would need to use `.ltr` and `.rtl` classes where you need to change the direction. The snippet can be adapted to use `[dir]` attribute instead of classes, but RTLCSS configuration is easier with classes.

This is quite laborious, but that's not a case we aimed to handle when we started the RTL support.

I hope this help!

body:
Regarding generating an entire palette from one color, I don’t think we’ll ever do that ourselves. Not our style or approach with what we’re doing in Bootstrap.

But in regards to variables and customizations, that’s definitely something to improve. With v5.3.0, we’re trying to balance adding color mode support in v5, using more CSS variables, and still supporting a Sass based approach. The intention is to give even more customization options without the need for Sass, but that’s a little difficult given lack of browser support for color manipulation (e.g., lighten or darken that background-color 10% based on a foreground color).

The new color mode responsive utilities in v5.3.0 are also meant to paint a picture of what future updates to the theming and utilities will look like in v6, which we want to get onto next after v5.3.0 goes stable. We want core theme colors (`$theme-colors`) that aren’t necessarily tied to minimum color contrast as text again white background (e.g., green, teal, yellow, etc could all be better defaults if they didn’t have to be used as body text colors). And from those colors, we want more subtle options. The downside here is that Sass never allows for variable generation, so we can’t make more Sass variables without writing them ourselves. I digress though, lots of little challenges and always more we can do :).

Feel free to keep sharing ideas here, we’d love to hear them!
author: mdo

body:
Thanks for the reply (I'm not the OP), great to hear you're aware of possible improvements. I opened an issue outlining my current problem regarding how variables are defined: https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues/37949.

I think it would be great to extend the `$theme-colors`, which is currently possible, and kind-of supported, but it's usually not straightforward. I started using "var-var" variables in my project. You could consider doing something of the like in Bootstrap:

```scss
$primary-var: var(--bs-primary); // or interpolate with $prefix
```

The issue with SCSS variables is that if you use them in some (any?) places, overriding the corresponding CSS variable will do nothing. A different approach might be if you only used "var-var" variables as above, and users wouldn't have to redefine them, simply overriding the CSS variables at :root would suffice.

author: chalin

body:
No, I believe it is not possible to enable CSS Grid directly in your project's package.json file. CSS Grid is a feature of CSS itself and is not a separate package that can be installed or enabled through npm.

Bootstrap 5.2 does include the ability to enable or disable CSS Grid layout on a per-build basis by using the enable-grid Sass variable. This means that you can enable or disable CSS Grid when building Bootstrap by modifying this variable in your own Sass setup.

To enable CSS Grid in Bootstrap 5.2, you need to set the enable-grid variable to true in your Sass setup before importing Bootstrap. This will include the CSS Grid layout classes in the compiled CSS, allowing you to use them in your HTML:

`
$enable-grid: true;
`

author: denizgolbas

body:
# How to Target Bootstrap in PHP Files


1. Download the latest version of Bootstrap from the official website: https://getbootstrap.com/.
2. Unzip the downloaded file and copy the "css" and "js" folders to your project directory.
3. In your PHP file, add the following code to include the Bootstrap CSS file in the head section of your HTML:

   ```html
   <head>
       <link rel="stylesheet" href="css/bootstrap.min.css">
   </head>
4. Similarly, add the following code to include the Bootstrap JavaScript file at the end of your HTML body:
 ```
<body>
    <!-- your HTML markup goes here -->
    <script src="js/bootstrap.min.js"></script>
</body> 
```
5. Once you've included the Bootstrap files in your PHP file, you can start using Bootstrap classes and components to style your HTML elements.

author: capymind

body:
Hi there,

Those are standard comments, that's why. [RTLCSS](https://rtlcss.com/) uses them to output the RTL version.

If you want to drop those comments, you may do as Bootstrap currently does:
1. compile a non-minified CSS file, with comments — eg. `bootstrap.css`;
2. compile a minified, compressed CSS file without comments — eg. `bootstrap.min.css`;
3. run RTLCSS on the first file to get `bootstrap.rtl.css`, and optionally minify it on the go to `bootstrap.rtl.min.css` if you don't need the unminified version.

That's how RTLCSS works and how we do it at the moment in Bootstrap. Does it help?

body:
Yes, you can overlay the dismiss/close button on the image in Bootstrap 5 Modal by customizing the modal header. Instead of using the default modal title component, you can create a custom header that includes the close button and positions it over the image. Here's an example of how you can achieve this: ```html
``` In this example, we've created a custom modal header by using a close button with the `btn-close` class and positioning it absolutely with the `position-absolute` class and the utility classes `top-0` and `end-0` to place it at the top right corner of the modal. We've also added some margin with the `m-3` class to give some space around the button. In the modal body, we've added the image with the `img-fluid` class to make it responsive and take up the full width of the modal. By removing the default modal title component, you can avoid the empty spacing on top of the image. I hope this helps!

body: You'll find how to customize Bootstrap [in our documentation](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/customize). In a few words, you can use Sass and/or CSS variables.
author: julien-deramond
azizbecha
minhaj-313

body:
> There are two main ways you can override Bootstrap CSS:
> 
> Override using a higher specificity selector and properties via CSS Using Bootstrap Sass variables (recommended) We recommend using Sass variables because that will not override the regular CSS from Bootstrap, but in fact, change it altogether. However, I will still show you how you can override the Bootstrap CSS in a fast and easy way.
> 
> Overriding Bootstrap via CSS The most straightforward way of overriding Bootstrap styles is using plain old CSS. In this case, you will need to include your own CSS file after having Bootstrap included. Here’s an example:
> 
> After that, you can override the Bootstrap default styles in the custom.css file. For example, this is how you can change the color of the primary button: /* bootstrap.min.css */ .btn-primary { background-color: #FC7F66; border-color: #FC7F66; } In this case, the specificity is the same as for the default Bootstrap styles, but it is being overridden because it is written after the initial styles. Some Bootstrap selectors may have a higher specificity so you will have to write selectors that match that specificity or have a higher one. Here’s an example: /* bootstrap.min.css */ .navbar-light .navbar-brand { color: rgba(0,0,0,.9); } This is the default style for the branding element inside the navigation bar. If you want to override this you would need to write with the exact same specificity or a higher one: /* custom.css */ .navbar-light .navbar-brand { color: #FC7F66; } Customizing Bootstrap via Sass variables and mixins By far this is the best way to customize Bootstrap for your project because instead of overriding the styles it actually changes them saving you time and duplication of style declarations. The one downside is that you need to set up a local environment that can compile Sass files. The good news is that there are many ways to do this. Some time ago, we wrote a tutorial on how you can [set up a simple Gulp 4, Bootstrap Sass, and BrowserSync](https://themesberg.com/blog/tutorial/gulp-4-bootstrap-sass-browsersync) working environment. You can also just directly download the files to get started and resume this tutorial. If you followed the tutorial for setting up Bootstrap Sass, Gulp 4, and BrowserSync you will have a _variables.scss file where you can easily change styles such as colors, sizing, shadows that will propagate throughout your whole Bootstrap application. This means that if you change the $primary variable to your preferred color, all of the primary classes from Bootstrap will take on the new $primary color, including the buttons, badges, navigation items, and so on. This is one of the reasons that using variables is much easier once you get used to them because you won’t have to override each class one by one using classic CSS. Bootstrap themes and templates You can save a lot of time and benefit from professionally designed templates by downloading or purchasing some of our free and premium [Bootstrap templates, themes, and UI kits](https://themesberg.com/templates/bootstrap) from Themesberg. Each theme uses the recommended Gulp, Bootstrap Sass, and BrowserSync stack environment. Our newest themes that use Bootstrap 5 also no longer require jQuery as a dependency, rendering your website faster and easier to work with.

Thanks @minhaj-313

I was testing cursor editor, I'm not into this ai stuff. When asked to create modal and custom function it did what I paste,
Anyway before using ai editor I did same as it.

To improve docs page add example of using javascript for already existing modals

```js
bootstrap.Modal.getInstance(document.getElementById('exampleModal')).hide();
```
```js
bootstrap.Modal.getInstance('#exampleModal').hide();
```

and for not yet initiated modals

```js
bootstrap.Modal.getOrCreateInstance(document.getElementById('exampleModal')).show();
```

```js
bootstrap.Modal.getOrCreateInstance('#exampleModal').show();
```

cheers

Ah yes, `$list-group-item-color-scale` in Bootstrap v5.2.3 is used alongside `shift-color` which uses `tint-color`. Indeed `0` not being exactly `0%`, it can cause errors like the one you had. Tricky use cases for some Sass vars that can be customized.
Glad you found a solution. Don't hesitate if you find other issues.

body: I've found the root cause of this edge-case behavior. It only happens when you enable font scaling in system properties with non-integer values. In my case, I use Ubuntu 23.04 with Chrome on a 4K display, and my font scaling was 1,2x. It looks as it should when I set scaling to 1,0x or 2,0x. Also, it seems that Firefox doesn't respect system-wide font scaling.

body:
Without knowing your code, it's hard to find a correct answer. Basically, there are several methods for your request:

1. Process the form via AJAX, so you have no page reload
2. Or store the "active Tab" in Cookie or localStorage
3. Or use the hash to open a specific tab4. 

From the Docs (You can activate individual tabs in several ways):
https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/components/navs-tabs/#via-javascript

You can check the first and second example from this post for localStorage- / hash-solution (Both variants use jQuery.):
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/18999501/how-can-i-keep-selected-bootstrap-tab-on-page-refresh
author: patkon

body:

This is the default behavior of the browsers (see https://www.w3schools.com/tags/tryit.asp?filename=tryhtml_select_multiple or https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Attributes/multiple#try_it).

From one of these links:
> When `multiple` is specified, most browsers will show a scrolling list box instead of a single-line dropdown.

body:
As in most Open Source projects/repositories, you can find Contributing Guidelines directly in the repo. Ours is available [here](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/main/.github/CONTRIBUTING.md); it includes a section that explains how you can clone the project locally.

Then, in our `README.md`, you can learn [how to run the documentation locally](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/tree/main#running-documentation-locally).

You can start with these elements. And then you'll have to understand the logic of the framework internally to be able to resolve https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues/38569 (which might not be the easiest btw because the maintainers have not yet analyzed it).

Unfortunately, without further elements from your own code or the theme used, I won't be able to help more than saying that `$background-color` doesn't exist in Bootstrap so the problem is probably elsewhere.

In order to just fix the Sass compilation, you can just try to define a random value (e.g. yellow) for this var and check what's the impact of this modification 🤷

$background-color: #fd0;
```

body:
I've created a Bootstrap 5 Migrate Tool command-line script which scans all pages/templates in a folder and:

- Replaces all the Bootstrap 4 classes with Bootstrap 5 classes.
- Renames all the data attributes (such as data-toggle and data-target) to the new bs-prefixed names
- Replaces deprecated components (such as `.jumbotron` and `.media`) with the relevant utility classes
- Updates all Bootstrap v4 CDN links (jsdelivr.net, unpkg.com and stackpath.bootstrapcdn.com) to latest version of Bootstrap v5.

It's free and open-source: https://github.com/coliff/bootstrap-5-migrate-tool

body:
[Color modes](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/customize/color-modes/#dark-mode) documentation mentions that
> Color modes can be toggled globally on the `<html>` element, or on specific components and elements, thanks to the `data-bs-theme` attribute

So if you are using a color picker, I imagine that you'll switch between light and dark mode. In this case, you can just add `data-bs-theme="auto"` on the `<html>` element. The JS color modes script example will update this value based on user actions and/or configuration.

FYI, we provide a [color modes example](https://github.com/twbs/examples/tree/main/color-modes), and the corresponding [index.html](https://github.com/twbs/examples/tree/main/color-modes/index.html) if it can help.

author: coudrak

body:
We already use PostCSS, and BTW autoprefixer cannot work outside PostCSS anymore.

Modern CSS cannot replace Sass, since it's meant to improve worklow **before** CSS is served. Think of functions, mixins, maps or lists: this won't land in CSS and are quite useful for us.

Take a look at our utilities API or the grid framework: there's no way standard CSS can help us as much as Sass does.

On the other hand, we're already levearaging modern CSS features like custom properties, but it needs to be slow: browser support needs to be really, really good and we have to implement things without breaking things.

All those CSS shiny things will be used un Bootstrap, if they make sense.

body:
`$body-bg` defines the body background color but is also reused to define the default background color of most of the components too. Think of it as the default background color for everything.
From that, you can override specifically the other background colors like `$dropdown-bg`, `$accordion-bg`, `$offcanvas-bg-color`, etc.
If you want only to change the background color of the body, you probably just need to apply a `.bg-danger` (for example)  to the `<body>` HTML element.

body: If you use [the media query color mode type](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/customize/color-modes/#building-with-sass), you may use the `` element to provide different sources depending on media queries, eg.: ```html ``` See [the WebKit blog post for dark mode announcement back in 2019](https://webkit.org/blog/8840/dark-mode-support-in-webkit/). If you use themes based on `data-bs-theme`, I’m afraid there's no really good solution: 1. either use JavaScript to check for `data-bs-theme` value and switch source accordingly, 2. or add every sources in your markup from the start, and use CSS to hide them (eg. `[data-bs-theme="dark"] img[src*="light"] { display: none; }`). As of now, there's no straightforward way to accomplish this with Bootstrap only.

body:
Hey @Awazleon

You're using the right approach. However, it does not work yet in all cases for components that way depending on how they are developed.
Here, it's because we use `filter: $carousel-dark-control-icon-filter;` (a Sass var) in our code which produces in your use case a static CSS rule (not dynamic with CSS variables):

```css
[data-bs-theme=dark] .carousel .carousel-control-prev-icon,
[data-bs-theme=dark] .carousel .carousel-control-next-icon, [data-bs-theme=dark].carousel .carousel-control-prev-icon,
[data-bs-theme=dark].carousel .carousel-control-next-icon {
  filter: invert(1) grayscale(100);
}
```

We don't have explicit `[data-bs-theme=light] .carousel*` rules so the dark theme will be always displayed even if there are contextual light theme components within.

As a patch, I think you could add something like the following CSS rule (or a more precise one) to your project to handle the color of the indicators (reverting the applied filter):

```css
[data-bs-theme="light"] .carousel .carousel-control-prev-icon,
[data-bs-theme="light"] .carousel .carousel-control-next-icon,
[data-bs-theme="light"].carousel .carousel-control-prev-icon,
[data-bs-theme="light"].carousel .carousel-control-next-icon {
  filter: none;
}
```

/cc @mdo for info about this use case. We might reference this issue somewhere for the next batch of modifications regarding color modes.

body:
Hey @3101ajeet 

I've converted your issue to a discussion since it's probably rather a usage problem than an actual issue in Bootstrap.

By default, Bootstrap is in LTR (e.g. https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/getting-started/introduction/).
RTL is handled separately and requires extra steps: https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/getting-started/rtl/#required-html.

Based on the few elements I have in your use case, I'd say that you can:
- Remove dir="rtl" on the `<html>` element
- Set `lang="en"`, on the `<html>` element.
- Load `bootstrap.min.css` instead of `bootstrap.rtl.min.css`

body:
Hey,

For the dropdown, since you're in a nvavbar place `.position-absolute` on the dropdown menu to force it absolute and not static in mobile breakpoitns.

For the icons:
- To get them inline, just add `.flex-row` on `.navbar-nav`
- To get them always in the top-right corner, the easiest solution would be to add `.position-absolute end-0` to the icons container. Far from ideal and a bit messy but might do the trick. Otherwise maybe try something using flexboxes and order property.

Regards.

body:
I don't think you will be happy with inline styles like
`<h2 style="color: #FFFFFF;font-size: 1vw;">`

So actually the headings are already responsive in Bootstrap. If you want to change the sizes, you have to look into the Sass variables:
https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/content/typography/#sass-variables

Especially the H2 is determined in the file _variabless.scss (Line 643):
https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/main/scss/_variables.scss#L634

And here you can find the explanation of how the responsive font sizes work in bootstrap
https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/getting-started/rfs/

body:
You can use the `Bootlint` tool to scan your website's HTML files and detect issues with Bootstrap usage. Install Bootlint using npm and run it with the command `bootlint your-html-file.html`, replacing your-html-file.html with the path to the HTML file you want to check. This will help you identify common mistakes and deviations from Bootstrap's guidelines in the HTML code.

Please note that Bootlint focuses on HTML usage, and for CSS-related issues, you may need to use a separate CSS linting tool like `stylelint` to enforce Bootstrap guidelines and detect deviations in CSS code.

For bootstrap, you can use `stylelint-config-twbs-bootstrap` package.

body:
Hello! It's great to hear that you want to contribute and get involved in open-source projects. Contributing to open-source can be a rewarding experience, and it's fantastic that you're willing to help.

Here are some steps and tips to help you find a suitable project and get started with contributing:

    Choose a Project: Start by selecting a project that aligns with your interests and expertise. Look for projects that you are passionate about and are actively maintained. You can search on popular code hosting platforms like GitHub for projects that are tagged as "beginner-friendly" or "good first issue." These issues are usually intended for newcomers and are relatively easy to get started with.

    Understand the Project: Once you've identified a project, take some time to explore its codebase, documentation, and contribution guidelines. Understanding how the project is structured and operates is crucial for effective contributions.

    Join the Community: Many open-source projects have active communities on platforms like GitHub, GitLab, or Discord. Join their communication channels, such as chat rooms or mailing lists, to introduce yourself, ask questions, and get to know the developers and contributors.

    Review Existing Issues: Check the project's issue tracker or repository for open issues. Look for issues labeled "help wanted," "good first issue," or "beginner-friendly." These are often a good starting point for newcomers.

    Ask for Guidance: If you're having trouble finding an issue or understanding how to contribute, don't hesitate to ask for help from the community or maintainers. They will likely be more than happy to guide you in the right direction.

    Start Small: When contributing to a project for the first time, start with small and manageable tasks. It could be fixing a typo in documentation, adding a simple feature, or writing tests for existing code. This will help you get familiar with the project's workflow and coding style.

    Follow Contribution Guidelines: Each project may have its own set of contribution guidelines, coding standards, and processes. Make sure to read and follow these guidelines to ensure your contributions are accepted smoothly.

    Test Your Changes: Before submitting a pull request, test your changes thoroughly to ensure they work as expected and do not introduce new bugs.

    Submit a Pull Request: Once you've made your contribution, submit a pull request (PR) with a clear description of what you've changed and why. Be patient and open to feedback from the maintainers.

    Keep Learning: Open-source contributions are a continuous learning process. Embrace feedback and keep improving your skills as you contribute to more projects.

Remember, it's normal to face challenges as a newcomer, but don't get discouraged. Everyone starts somewhere, and the open-source community is generally supportive and welcoming to newcomers. Happy contributing!

author: julien-deramond

body:
To use Bootstrap 5.3.1 in your frontend project, you don't need to manually download and paste the files in your project folder. Instead, you can include Bootstrap directly in your HTML file using either a CDN or by installing it through npm.

1. Using CDN (Content Delivery Network):
You can include the Bootstrap CSS and JavaScript files directly in your HTML file using the following code in the head section:

```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
  <title>Your Page Title</title>
  <link href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.3.1/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css" rel="stylesheet">
</head>
<body>
  <!-- Your HTML content here -->
  
  <script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.3.1/dist/js/bootstrap.bundle.min.js"></script>
</body>
</html>
```

2. Using npm (Node Package Manager):
If you prefer to install Bootstrap through npm, follow these steps:

a. Open a terminal in your project folder and run the following command to install Bootstrap:

```bash
npm install bootstrap@5.3.1
```

b. After the installation, include the Bootstrap CSS and JavaScript files in your HTML file like this:

```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
  <title>Your Page Title</title>
  <link href="node_modules/bootstrap/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css" rel="stylesheet">
</head>
<body>
  <!-- Your HTML content here -->
  
  <script src="node_modules/bootstrap/dist/js/bootstrap.bundle.min.js"></script>
</body>
</html>
```

Now you should be able to access and use Bootstrap 5.3.1 in your frontend project. You can start utilizing Bootstrap classes and components in your HTML to style and enhance your web pages.

> I know it's open source and it have a gigantic community, but it all started under Twitter umbrella, now that Elon is in charge, mdo and fat working on another project.

Bootstrap has been independent of Twitter for some years now and mdo is still working on the project in his spare time, like all the maintainers of Bootstrap.

> Everything I see these days are build with Tailwind CSS. Since 2023 Tailwind have take the lead.

Regarding Tailwind, both have a different approach and are different tools so folks use the tools they think are the most appropriate to their needs. A lot of companies still use Bootstrap.

> But I wonder if this is the end of an era.

IDK but we're still putting effort into maintaining Bootstrap.

body:
Hello @archnakj 

> Is there a master list of classes for bootstrap 5.3 available somewhere?

Unfortunately no, but I started https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/37076 a few months ago to include a glossary in the documentation. I hope that I'll be able to finish it one day.

> This file is not readable (at least by me!). Should I be including this in my project? The main bootstrap page does not recommend this file be included.

I confirm that you should omit this `docs.css` in your project. We include it in our StackBlitz examples because sometimes it's needed for some extra styles to display some icons or to render correctly some grid elements for instance. But for the navbars, you shouldn't need it. I tried a few examples in StackBlitz that seem to work correctly without this `docs.css` within StackBlitz; which ones are problematic for you?

import 'bootstrap/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css'

app.use(createPinia())
app.use(router)

app.mount('#app')
```

body:
Hi @stsrki,

As far as I understand, those are just examples to show how we can make a heading (doesn't have to be an `h1`) more prominent / stand out by using `display-*` classes.

_When you need a heading to stand out, consider using a display heading—a larger, slightly more opinionated heading style._

And if you are concerned about Bootstrap's SEO, if you inspect those examples, you can see those are `<div>`s not headings.

body:
Dropdowns are managed by popper.
You therefore need to register them via javascript.

The easiest way is to add data attributes and let the Bootstrap script do it. See:
https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/components/dropdowns/#via-data-attributes

Note: if I'm right, `bootstrap.bundle.min.js` is the bundle of `popper.min.js` and `bootstrap.min.js`. You should have one or the other but not the two.

body:
I'd say it's safe to use `unpkg.com` instead of `cdn.jsdelivr.net`.
For example, https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.3.1/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css becomes https://unpkg.com/bootstrap@5.3.1/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css (`integrity` sha384 value stays the same)
Don't hesitate to tell me if it works better.
If so, I'll try to create a PR to talk about this workaround in our documentation.

We are planning to refactor how the themes work in v6 so that it's way easier to customize the themes (changing, adding colors globally, or more closely to the components, etc.). We tried things in https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/customize/color-modes/ and https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/customize/sass/ but it's prolly not enough.

body:
A migration from Bootstrap v3 to v5 might be tricky because of the 2 major versions difference, but it is possible.
You can refer to the following migration guides to spot the main differences:
- [Migrating to v4](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/4.6/migration/)
- [Migrating to v5](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/migration/)

For more detail on specific points, you might want to refer to the release notes of the intermediate versions too: https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/releases.

However, I wouldn't use the v4 as a transition to avoid having to do a double migration. I would try to target the latest Bootstrap version available.
Good luck with this work @kushgup123
author: julien-deramond

body:
* `root` is required: it's where you can find low-level custom properties defined in `:root` (e.g. `--bs-body-color`, `--bs-body-bg`, etc.)
* `utilities` is only needed if you also import `utilities/api` which generates utilities (e.g. `.m-0`, `.p-3`, `.position-fixed`, etc.). So if you don't use Bootstrap utilities, it's not needed.
* `reboot` is labeled as optional but can be handy depending on your needs (see https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/content/reboot/)
* the rest depends on what you actually use in your project so optional
author: julien-deramond
picobots

body:
This is not the default rendering of the focused floating labels in dark mode (see https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/forms/floating-labels/). If this is the default rendering that you can see with v5.3.2, could you please try to create a reproducible example by using CodePen or Stackblitz so that we could fix it?

Anyway, if you want to fix it on your side, I think you can override the rendering when focused with such a rule (to adapt to your needs):

```css
.form-floating label::after {
  background-color: #fd0 !important; /* !important might not be needed depending on your use case */
}
```

Since it is handled by `::after`, you won't be able to use a class provided by Bootstrap such as `.bg-*`.

> This is not the default rendering of the focused floating labels in dark mode (see https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/forms/floating-labels/). If this is the default rendering that you can see with v5.3.2, could you please try to create a reproducible example by using CodePen or Stackblitz so that we could fix it?
> 
> Anyway, if you want to fix it on your side, I think you can override the rendering when focused with such a rule (to adapt to your needs):
> 
> ```css
> .form-floating label::after {
>   background-color: #fd0 !important; /* !important might not be needed depending on your use case */
> }
> ```
> 
> Since it is handled by `::after`, you won't be able to use a class provided by Bootstrap such as `.bg-*`.
author: Kiyotaka-Ayanokoj

body:
@SapphireGirl This is impossible with Bootstrap (or any web framework for that matter). Bootstrap modals are just HTML DOM elements that look like modal windows, they're not actual windows. You can't drag individual HTML elements out of a web browser onto a second monitor. (One weird exception: You could technically make a single browser window extra wide so that it spans two monitors and then I suppose you could achieve this, but that would be a wildly unusual approach.)

In practice, the only way to achieve your second monitor goal would be moving the content in question to a new browser tab or popup window, and then the user can move that browser tab/window to a second monitor.

body:
Hello @stijn7621 
This is linked to the orientation of your form.
* For [horizontal forms](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/forms/layout/#horizontal-form), each row (with `.row`) contains the label and a form control, so the label needs a `.col-form-label` to have the right padding in order to be vertically aligned to the form control.
* For [vertical forms (by default in our doc)](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/forms/layout/#forms), `.form-label` is used.

body:
Hey @gsartori
To my knowledge, there's no interface in the JS to do that (with a `data-bs-something` for example) and define a specific target in your DOM as a container for your backdrop. 
As you can see in the following, `rootElement` is in the Offcanvas component black box and is not directly accessible.

```js
return new Backdrop({
  className: CLASS_NAME_BACKDROP,
  isVisible,
  isAnimated: true,
  rootElement: this._element.parentNode,
  clickCallback: isVisible ? clickCallback : null
})
```

Depending on what you're trying to achieve, you could maybe use the [static backdrop](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/components/offcanvas/#static-backdrop) and the modify the CSS rendering of `.offcanvas-backdrop` 🤷

body:
Just for better understanding: The SCSS files are not needed on the live page. With the SCSS files you can generate your own Bootstrap CSS file if that is needed (i.e. if you want to make changes to the default Bootstrap CSS).

If the page uses a custom Bootstrap version, you will of course need these SCSS files to extend or change the visual appearance of the page. So, if the page was generated with modified scss files.

To turn the SCSS files into a working Bootstrap CSS, you need additional software or similar.
I personally know and use only CodeKit - but there are many others.

Oh and I spotted an issue, you're using `data-ride`. It was named like this in Bootstrap v4 but in Bootstrap v5, all data attributes are named `data-bs-*`, so it's `data-bs-ride` for instance (double-check [this example](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.1/components/carousel/#with-controls)).

body:
Based on our current architecture in Bootstrap, I'll go with Sass since you seem to be able to have a custom build.
Our custom properties are built from Sass values. So for now, I'd say it's safer because maybe the `.nav-link` implementation could change in the future (not planned but could happen). If it changes, you won't know. With the Sass approach, you're pretty much sure that we will reuse this variable whatever the implementation is, and if this variable becomes deprecated, it's going to be mentioned in the migration guide or directly when you build your Sass files with a warning message.

body:
It seems like the issue you're facing is related to the positioning of the breadcrumbs. To ensure they don't overlap with the footer when scrolling, you can adjust their positioning using CSS. Here's an example of how you might solve this issue: ```html
  1. Home
  2. Project
``` And in your CSS file: ```css /* CSS */ .breadcrumbs { position: fixed; bottom: 0; width: 100%; background-color: white; /* Adjust background color if needed */ z-index: 999; /* Set a high z-index to ensure breadcrumbs appear above other elements */ } ``` This CSS code will fix the breadcrumbs to the bottom of the page, preventing them from overlapping with the footer while scrolling. Adjust the `background-color` and other styles as needed to match your website's design. This solution assumes that your footer is not fixed at the bottom of the viewport. If your footer is also fixed, additional adjustments might be necessary. from chatGpt

Basically I removed the `.d-flex` from the `.multi-collapse` `<div>`s and it seems to work:
- When you click to "Toggle both elements" button at desktop breakpoint, the blocks from the doc are shown but not what's in the navbar
- When you click to the toggle menu button at smaller breakpoint, both the blocks from the doc and the `.multi-collapse` `<div>`s from your example are shown

The rule `.collapse:not(.show)` in Bootstrap is equal to `display: none` so having `.d-flex` forces a display that makes them always visible.

body: Hi @m-reisner My guess, just by reading the code, is that you're using `` and I think you're mixing two different usages for icons (see https://icons.getbootstrap.com/#install). The JS that you're using is based on the `href` value so switching modes is working correctly. However, the rendering of the icons is based on the `.bi-*` classes. Since your dropdown is using ``, the JS will never change `.bi-circle-half` (it actually changes the `` which is not used for the rendering since it's handled by `.bi-circle-half`), so the rendered icon will always be the half circle. Said differently, right now, I suppose that when you click on the moon star icon in the dropdown, the rendered icon is probably still the half circle even if the HTML is probably `` (because `` value is changed but `.bi-circle-half` is not changed). If you want to stick with the `.bi-*` icons, you can probably get rid of all the `` in your HTML for the theme selector, and you should modify the JS to change the class dynamically instead of the `` values.

<?php
if ($_SERVER["REQUEST_METHOD"] == "POST") {
    // Get form data
    $name = $_POST["name"];

    // Set the recipient email address

    // Set the subject of the email

    // Build the email message
    $email_message = "Name: $name\n";
    $email_message .= "Email: $email\n";
    $email_message .= "Message:\n$message";

    // Send the email
    mail($to, $subject, $email_message);

    // Redirect back to the form with a success message
    header("Location: index.html?status=success");
} else {
    // Redirect back to the form with an error message
    header("Location: index.html?status=error");
}
?>

body:
Hi @Benoit-ROBIN,
For now, it's not really possible to override the value of some colors by setting them to CSS variables because we are still using Sass functions in the core Bootstrap code to calculate some other colors or to find some contrast ratio. This is why you have `SassError: $color: var(--my-custom-white) is not a color.`.
For some colors, it might work, but it depends on what you will override and how.
This is definitely something we'd like to improve in the v6.

author: dbraun-ascendium

body:
I don't have the whole source code so I will make some assumptions in my answer.

The table background color in `v5.3.0-alpha3`  was transparent and I can see in your video that you have overridden the `color` based on `--body-text` which doesn't come from Bootstrap. So all the colors that change are not from Bootstrap actually.
In `v5.3.0`, there's a solid background color for the tables so your actual system (not really based on Bootstrap for the bg and text colors) will only change what's outside of the table.

In `v5.3.0` and `v5.3.0-alpha3` the color modes rely on `data-bs-theme`. If you want only to react to the system's light and dark modes, you need to build Bootstrap with Sass and set `$color-mode-type: media-query;` (see https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/customize/color-modes/#building-with-sass).

Without recompiling Bootstrap, there will be another possibility in JS to simulate our "auto" mode in the documentation based on https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/main/site/static/docs/5.3/assets/js/color-modes.js but I wouldn't recommend this approach.

author: manuelmeurer

body:
Hey @manuelmeurer 

So right now, tweaking the `--bs-primary` CSS variable to give all components a new color isn't possible. You can see `--bs-primary` primarily as a read-only CSS variable reusable in your website to get the primary color. For a consistent global look and setting a new primary color, diving into Sass is the way to go.

However, as explained in the article, depending on the needs, it's possible to override the rendering of the components one by one by overriding their CSS variables.

Some improvements will be made when Bootstrap is refactored in v6 for an easier theming approach. The groundwork with CSS variables and color modes in v5.2/3 was the opening act, and we committed to keeping things smooth without any regressions.

author: Alex-Toucan

body:
Hi!
If you're working with an icon that is an image file (like a PNG or SVG), you can use CSS to change its color.

```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
  <style>
    .icon {
      fill: red; /* Change this to your desired color */
    }
  </style>
</head>
<body>
  <svg class="icon" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 24 24">
    <path d="M12 2C6.48 2 2 6.48 2 12s4.48 10 10 10 10-4.48 10-10S17.52 2 12 2zm-1 15h2v-2h-2zm0-4h2V7h-2z"/>
  </svg>
</body>
</html>
```

Copy and paste this code into an HTML file. You can change the fill property in the CSS to the color you desire.
author: abdullah26tr

body:
Hey @raj-oeeai 

IDK the source code of Tabler. I can see in your logs that there's an issue while calling the `divide()` function in `node_modules/bootstrap/scss/vendor/_rfs.scss`. In Bootstrap, that means that the 2 parameters have incompatible units. 

For example, in Bootstrap, if you do `$test: divide(.75em, 100%);` you'd have the same error.

In Tabler (https://github.com/tabler/tabler/blob/682eaa88a8c4df1b3a503d8f4090a425a418d6aa/src/scss/ui/_badges.scss#L8), there is: `min-width: ($badge-padding-y * 2 ) + (divide($badge-font-size, 100%) * 1em);`

So I'd say that probably `$badge-font-size` is set to `.75em` within the library or if you override it in your project. And that causes the issue.

Again I'm not familiar with Tabler code so the issue could come from anything else :)

Do you know for example if this is a recent modification on their side, or if it worked with a previous version of Bootstrap?
author: julien-deramond

body:
Hi @julien-deramond,

Initially, I had a smooth experience using Bootstrap "4.4.1" and Tabler-UI "0.0.34", but with their deprecation, I decided to update.
By implementing a workaround by adjusting the variables with compatible units, the issue is 'fixed' for me.

Hopefully, it might be helpful to others facing a similar situation.
(https://github.com/tabler/tabler/issues/1819#issuecomment-1926633325).
author: raj-oeeai

body: It’s true that many [developers ](https://glorymoto.com/collection/leather-motorcycle-shoes/)jump straight into coding with Bootstrap, especially for admin panels or simple sites. However, creating a design layout first can ensure a more polished result. Typically, designers start with wireframes or mockups (using tools like Figma) and consider Bootstrap's grid system, components, and breakpoints for responsiveness. A solid approach includes designing with Bootstrap's limitations and strengths in mind, then translating that into clean code. Balancing design and development early on will lead to better outcomes.

I've transformed your issue into a discussion @sinjuangajan.

> Where can I get the original RTL SCSS code for Bootstrap without it being compiled?

The SCSS code is actually the same as the one you can find in `node_modules/bootstrap/scss`. The RTL differences are present in the files with comments like for instance `/* rtl:begin:ignore */ .... /* rtl:end:ignore */`thanks to [RTLCSS](https://rtlcss.com).

Additional information can be found at https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/getting-started/rtl/.

body:
Hey @Stella521 

> Should I understand the classes using different formats for xs and (sm, md, lg, xl, and xxl) are "the remaining breakpoints"?

The "remaining breakpoints" are all breakpoints except `xs`, so `sm`, `md`, `lg`, `xl` and `xxl`.

For example:
* `mt-0` (that corresponds to `xs`) has no abbreviation and is defined without any CSS media
* `mt-sm-0` is defined from `sm` with the following CSS media: `@media (min-width: 576px)`
* `mt-md-0` is defined from `md` with the following CSS media: `@media (min-width: 768px)`
* `mt-lg-0` is defined from `lg` with the following CSS media: `@media (min-width: 992px)`
* `mt-xl-0` is defined from `xl` with the following CSS media: `@media (min-width: 1200px)`
* `mt-xl-0` is defined from `xl` with the following CSS media: `@media (min-width: 1400px)`

I didn‘t mean to include it in Bootstrap by default, it would not be wise and I don‘t recommend it. Not only because of the breaking change, but also because this is a solution for a specific group of users that want to achieve a specific goal. 🙂

My proposal was to mention it in the `LTR and RTL at the same time` section of the documentation so users that want to achieve that could do it not with a workaround but with a tool that does all the work automatically and covering several edge cases and issues that come with the merge of both directions in a single file.

In 4.3.1 it was:

```html
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@4.3.1/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css" integrity="sha384-ggOyR0iXCbMQv3Xipma34MD+dH/1fQ784/j6cY/iJTQUOhcWr7x9JvoRxT2MZw1T" crossorigin="anonymous">
<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@4.3.1/dist/js/bootstrap.min.js" integrity="sha384-JjSmVgyd0p3pXB1rRibZUAYoIIy6OrQ6VrjIEaFf/nJGzIxFDsf4x0xIM+B07jRM" crossorigin="anonymous"></script>
```

In 5.3.3 it's:

```html
<link href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.3.3/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css" rel="stylesheet" integrity="sha384-QWTKZyjpPEjISv5WaRU9OFeRpok6YctnYmDr5pNlyT2bRjXh0JMhjY6hW+ALEwIH" crossorigin="anonymous">

```

The structure hasn't changed.
Maybe the integrity values are not the right ones on your side.

author: TheDogHusky

body:
I have converted your issue to a discussion first @TheDogHusky 
Our `.bg-*` classes are generated from the `$theme-colors` map. In your example, you override the `$theme-colors` and don't define the `dark`key so `.bg-dark`won't be generated. I also means that your `$theme-colors` will generated a `.bg-light-hes-blue` class for instance.

So in order to make `.bg-dark` available, you can add the following:

```scss
$theme-colors: (
  // ...
  "dark": $dark
);
```

And to have access to `$dark`, you'll need to import `variables.scss` before defining the map.

Hope it can help!

body:
I don't think you can exclude everything that is font-related, because there are a lot of `font-*` rules defined at different levels of the framework.

However, these `font-*` rules depends on Sass/CSS variables. You could search for them and apply, IDK, a `inherit` value to them so that it can be propagated everywhere.

If you want absolutely no `font-*` rules in your final CSS bundle, maybe applying a post-processing treatment on the file to search and remove these rules could work too.
