I would suspect that something in your project's environment applies things to your CSS files because this something detects that there's a URL and wants to force a prefix: `https://www.mydomain.tld/skin/frontend/default/custom_theme/css/`.

However, what's weird is that it also messes up a part of the URL where the original is `...svg+xml,%3csvg xmlns=...` and it transforms it to `...svg+xml,%3csv")xmlns=...` where the `g ` becomes a `")`.

I don't know OpenMage, maybe it does transformations by itself when manipulating files by detecting and transforming URLs.

body: Hi @Andre-23889 I transformed your issue into a Q&A discussion. This issue in the documentation is in the backlog somewhere, and I've even created some PRs recently to try to tackle it. Basically, we use SVG references to ease the maintenance of our documentation, but on the other hand, it can be difficult to get how it works for the users. In your real-life project, you won't import `https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/assets/css/docs.css` and instead you can do that: ```html Icon link ``` And you can replace the `.bi` by the following properties, for instance: ```css width="1rem" height="1rem" fill="currentcolor" ``` In the same spirit, you can embed the SVG directly within the `` without reference: ```html Icon link ``` And they are other ways to include/install the Bootstrap icons described in https://icons.getbootstrap.com/#install.

body:
Looks like a good approach to me! Currently the btn-/color/ classes don't respect their respective color-variables which is a known [issue](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues/38788).
I tried something similar where I pass a reference to the variable (--bs-primary) instead of the interpreted hex-code so I can calculate the lighter/darker nuances in realtime by using a relative-HSL-format.

It looks like this on the rendered page:
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/54bcdaf6-3e31-4e2c-9ac6-064dc59250a4)

The biggest challenge in this approach is color-contrast. I don't think you can calculate that in realtime with native CSS right now so we need a preprocessor, or Javasript, or have the developer manually set a contrast-color.

I hope some variant of this makes it into Bootstrap 6 :)
author: felixKessler

body:
Hi @MarkoOleksiyenko 

I'd say it makes sense. TBH, on a more general approach, I don't know yet what level of customization we will want to let to the users in the end. With the Sass variables, it was kind of free since it didn't generate necessarily too much extra CSS; with the CSS variables it might not be that free if everything becomes customizable.

Anyway, for this specific use case, I wouldn't mind providing 2 extra variables to handle that.
We already have:
- `--bs-accordion-btn-color`
- `--bs-accordion-btn-bg`
- `--bs-accordion-active-color`
- `--bs-accordion-active-bg`

For information, on a fork of Bootstrap that I maintain, we have added `--bs-accordion-btn-hover-bg`.

So, we could add in Bootstrap:
- `--bs-accordion-btn-hover-bg`
- `--bs-accordion-btn-hover-color`
- and maybe some others for the _::after_ arrow.

Any opinion @twbs/css-review?
If you're OK with that approach, I could create an issue or a PR, and plan it for maybe v5.4.0.
author: julien-deramond

body:
Hey there,

IMO, we could add `--bs-accordion-btn-hover-bg` and `--bs-accordion-btn-hover-color` quite easily and defaulting to actual. 
On the other side, I don't think we should add any `::after` variables now, since it means a copy paste of the svg. IMO, we should firstly change the way to manage the svg by using a `mask-image` and control it via `background-color`.

On my side will check the `mask-image` approach to play around and see the impact.

And yeah, having more customizable accordion would be a great addition for v5.4.0!

Cheers!

body:
From our `package-lock.json`, it can be seen that `axios` is a dependency coming from `bundlewatch`:

```json
"node_modules/bundlewatch": {
      "version": "0.3.3",
      "resolved": "https://registry.npmjs.org/bundlewatch/-/bundlewatch-0.3.3.tgz",
      "integrity": "sha512-qzSVWrZyyWXa546JpAPRPTFmnXms9YNVnfzB05DRJKmN6wRRa7SkxE4OgKQmbAY74Z6CM2mKAc6vwvd2R+1lUQ==",
      "dev": true,
      "dependencies": {
        "axios": "^0.24.0",
 ```
 
[Bundlewatch](https://bundlewatch.io) is only used by our CI (or manually locally) to checks file sizes, ensuring bundled browser assets don't jump in file size; it's not embedded into the Bootstrap bundle files.
IMO, there's no need on your end to fix this issue.

As of my knowledge, Bootstrap does not affect ID's so if an element has both classes and id, it is perfectly fine. Keep in mind though, that Bootstrap classes will overrun any css code you will put, unless you use the "!important" property on your css rule.

I am happy to help, feel free to reply if you have further questions about this topic!

author: lanesCSO

body: Prefixes are dictated by our `.browserslistrc` file and ensure that Bootstrap CSS file works and has the same rendering for all these combinations of browsers/OSs. Autoprefixer uses this file to add automatically the vendor prefixes unique to that browser or version. Without autoprefixer, some renderings could be broken on some combinations of browsers/OSs as some rules will be missing.

body:
Hello @palmem29,

> this does create a conformance issue in the markup.

Is it an issue spotted by a tool (or a human) while analyzing the markup statically, or is it causing any issues for end-users while they navigate, for instance with screen readers?

I checked in the history of the repo, this was integrated a while ago, included in https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/30936 following actions and discussions in https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/30755 if it can help to understand why it was done like this.
author: julien-deramond

body:
Yes it's an issue reported by a tool that is just scanning markup. I'm sure it works perfectly as intended when actually put to use by a user that benefits from these conformance standards.

When faced with those issues we can either a) contribute some code to make them go away or b) we can declare the issue to be a false positive. With option a: of course we'd love it if we didn't have to tack on to bootstrap just to appease the conformance crawler. With option b: lawyers can use such a crawler to drum up business and even if it's not a valid conformance concern: cost a client some $ after we've declared "false positive". We hope to have out clients' sites free of as many false positives as possible for this reason.

I don't exactly follow the conversation justifying the adding of the role attribute dynamically instead of including it statically in the markup. Could you elaborate?
author: palmem29

body:
Thanks for the precision, I can understand how it can impact this use case.

> I don't exactly follow the conversation justifying the adding of the role attribute dynamically instead of including it statically in the markup. Could you elaborate?

From what I understand from the discussion back then, and maybe @patrickhlauke has more elements to share or to rectify from what I'm saying here, it seems to have been done mostly to ensure that this role isn't forgotten in the markup by folks since it's mandatory.

I haven't dug into this topic too much, but by reading the current algorithm, maybe we could do both approaches with a hybrid version:
1. By default, adding the role (current behavior)
2. However, if `role="dialog"` was already there in the markup before the JS is executed, we don't remove it afterward. I haven't tested it but should be probably doable. This seems to be what's missing on your side currently:
    > If I were to add a role to the markup, closing the element would just remove it.
    
It wouldn't have any impacts on current usages but would allow adding `role="dialog"` in the markup so that scanning tools are happy.
author: julien-deramond

body: @julien-deramond, that would be awesome! I'm really humbled to find out Bootstrap decision makers respond to and consider input so quickly. I understand if the input isn't folded in today, tomorrow, this month, etc. But just to be heard and understood this quickly is: yeah, that's the word: humbling. That would be a perfect adjustment for how we leverage Bootstrap modals and offcanvas.

author: shivanraptor

body:
The error [NET: ERR_CERT_COMMON_NAME_INVALID](https://certera.com/kb/how-to-fix-err_cert_common_name_invalid-in-chrome-ssl-common-name-mismatch-error/) is commonly known as SSL common name mismatch error. This error arises from a misconfiguration of certificates on a server, and it can disrupt the secure connection between a user and a website. Beyond its technical origins, this error can also be triggered by various factors such as third-party extensions, security programs like antivirus and firewalls, and more. Different factors can lead to this error some are as follows: - 

1) Improper HTTPS Transition
2) Unrecognized Self-signed SSL
3) Antivirus Interference
4) Browser Extension Impact
5) Misconfigured Proxy settings

There are different ways to fix the error, you need to understand properly in detail, as I was also facing such an error and got a proper detailed guide for the solution. Hope it helps!

body:
Hello @imagoiq 

> "Form labels now require .form-label.". 
> ...
> So now I'm totally confused. Should I report a bug for the issue above? Is the doc page missing the .form-label? Is .form-floating label compatible with .form-label?

`.form-label` are used for regular form controls (e.g. https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/forms/form-control/). Floating labels are particular and don't require any extra `.form-label` class since it's handled directly by `.form-floating` put on the surrounding `<div>`.

> I also encountered a bug with [Readonly plaintext](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/forms/floating-labels/#readonly-plaintext) where if you change the $input-bg variable to something different from the body background, the label background is visible.

I'm not entirely sure whether this is an issue, it seems rather consistent and acceptable to have the label background visible here.

In our [Nav and tabs documentation](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/components/navs-tabs/):
* The entire first part of the documentation shows the effect of classes and DOM structure on the rendering, explains the concepts, but doesn't do anything dynamically. The developer has to switch the elements programmatically (e.g. `.active` class, `aria-selected`, etc.)
* The only dynamic part is explained in [JavaScript behavior](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/components/navs-tabs/#javascript-behavior), and requires JavaScript.

If you think there's a use case where it doesn't work, you can create a new issue and integrate a reproducible live example with CodePen, StackBlitz, or any other tool, to make that more understandable :)

It's not a Bootstrap bug as mentioned in https://github.com/orgs/twbs/discussions/40576#discussioncomment-9858213, and so https://drafts.fxtf.org/filter-effects/#FilterProperty:

> A value other than none for the [filter](https://drafts.fxtf.org/filter-effects/#propdef-filter) property results in the creation of a [containing block](https://drafts.csswg.org/css-display-4/#containing-block) for absolute and fixed positioned descendants unless the element it applies to is a document root element in the current [browsing context](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/document-sequences.html#browsing-context). The list of functions are applied in the order provided.

So I've transformed this issue to a GitHub discussion if it can help folks in the future :)

body:
Currently, we aim to provide a list of references for Bootstrap users who want to integrate Bootstrap with the main three frameworks: Angular, React, and Vue. For Vue, we already have [BootstrapVue](https://bootstrap-vue.org/) for Bootstrap 4 and [BootstrapVueNext](https://bootstrap-vue-next.github.io/bootstrap-vue-next/) for Vue 3, which is currently in alpha.

While I'm not fully familiar with the entire Vue ecosystem, we are always open to new and valuable contributions. If your Nuxt module gains traction and becomes a go-to reference for Vue users, we would certainly consider adding it to our list. At this moment, feel free to ping me around here :)

author: julien-deramond

body:
Hi @carlosmintfan 

Why two different licenses have been chosen is a question to ask to the authors, but I think in this case it does make sense:

MIT is a license made for software. It does include "and associated documentation" in the license text, but this would still be awkward should someone want to use only the documentation someplace else, as there wouldn't be any software associated with it.

CC licenses, except for CC0, can technically be used for software, but this is explicitly discouraged (see https://creativecommons.org/faq/#Can_I_use_a_Creative_Commons_license_for_software.3F).

So, not knowing which part users might want to re-use (software, docs, possibly both), it is a general recommendation to use a license made for software for that, and a license made for data for documentation. Sometimes what is what is not that easy to distinguish, but that is another matter. In terms of open source, the general recommendation is "a license approved by OSI or FSF" for software and a CC-license for everything else. Which of those depends on your specific needs and wishes.

As to CSS: CSS defines properties of a document. I would in general not count that as software: CSS can't be "executed".
author: knarrff

body:
Hi @knarrff 
Yeah, they should change the license of their Color modes toggler and the CSS to MIT.
Stop that precision, of course CSS is software! 😀 
And, in my opinion, docs should be MIT. If somebody wants to reuse the docs, just put the MIT license text and copyright info in the imprint, state that the docs from this and that project have been used, and nothing else. Maybe MIT needs more interpretation than CC when it comes to courts, but...why do we need that! And as the software is also MIT...just go with MIT.
And if I'm right, it's the same with Apache: Software is Apache license, docs are Apache license.

body:
I've created a [CodePen](https://codepen.io/julien-deramond/pen/poXyxzB) to visualize the differences and serve as a reference.

To display normal text, such as paragraphs, articles, long sections, etc. you’ll typically use `.text-body`. If you need lighter text, you can use `.text-body-secondary`, and for even lighter text, `.text-body-tertiary`. Even if you customize Bootstrap, it's likely that `.text-body-*` will remain shades of black in light mode.

However, the concepts of primary and secondary colors are different. These terms are used to define your brand’s primary and secondary colors. In Bootstrap, primary is blue, and secondary is black. In my example, if you want to create a tag, you’ll have a primary tag with a subtle primary background, primary border, and primary text. The same applies to your secondary color, which corresponds to the `text-secondary` color.

This can be confusing for users who do not customize Bootstrap, as the secondary color is gray and often used for regular text. However, for a different brand, the secondary color could be yellow. In that case, your `text-body-*` classes would be black and gray (in light mode), while `text-secondary` would be yellow.

The semantics are important.

Additionally, in our current implementation, `text-primary` and `text-secondary` do not change colors between light and dark modes, so the accessibility color contrast will differ from `text-body-*` on a `bg-body` background.
author: julien-deramond

body:
Thank you for your detailed explanation! I still have some questions:
1. Is text without classes the same as .text-body?
2. And then there's this bg-light class (yes, bg, not text). It seems to me that it's the same as bg-body-secondary (if not tertiary, I don't know right now); however it looks stupid in dark mode. So is it recommended to always use bg-body-secondary/tertiary instead of bg-light? Why do we even have both options - I think the body classes were added later, right?
author: carlosmintfan

body:
> 1. Is text without classes the same as .text-body?

By default, we define `<body>` to have `color: var(--bs-body-color)` which is the same color used by `.text-body`. So, generally speaking, text without additional classes will have the same color as `.text-body`, adapting to both light and dark modes. However, depending on your interface's complexity, you might need to apply these color rules within a specific sub-div, making the `.text-body` class useful in those scenarios.

> 2. And then there's this bg-light class [...] it looks stupid in dark mode

Classes like `.bg-light` and `.bg-dark` behave similarly to the primary, secondary, and success concepts. This means their colors remain constant regardless of light or dark mode. In contrast, `.bg-body*` classes adjust their colors to suit both light and dark modes.

> So is it recommended to always use bg-body-secondary/tertiary instead of bg-light? Why do we even have both options

The choice depends on your use cases across different color modes. If you don't need to accommodate color mode changes, you can use basic classes like `.bg-light`. However, if your interface needs to adapt to light and dark modes, using classes like `.bg-body`, `.bg-body-secondary` or `.bg-body-tertiary` is more appropriate.

Hello @victorolweny 

My 2 cents: if you want to contribute to Bootstrap, it might be easier to clone the repository rather than doing it like that.

Back to the question, I've just tried the scripts in the description and I don't have any issues after running `npm run download` and then `npm run postdownload` with the following configuration:

* Node v20.15.0
* npm 10.7.0
* macOS Sonoma 14.5

I tried with Node 22.5.1 and your configuration, and it works too on my machine.

It looks like you're having an issue with the `npm i` when running it in `bootstrap-5.2.3/`. Could be a network (configuration) issue or a npm configuration issue (with or without proxies, VPN, etc.).

I'm saying that because `rimraf` is actually a peer dependency that is installed by `npm i` (should be in `bootstrap-5.2.3/node_modules/.bin/rimraf`). So if there are download/network errors for some packages while running `npm i`, it could cause that.

If you go to `bootstrap-5.2.3/` and run manually `npm i`, do you have the same error?

> But I'm sure that the recent CVEs published in July (except if their content change at some point), don't affect Bootstrap 5. The corresponding versions can be found each time in the Hero Devs links.

Could you maybe explain why version 5 isn't affected? CVE-2024-6531 and CVE-2024-6484 are pretty much the same issue but it doesn't seem very different in 5. If I modify the proof of concept to bootstrap 5.3.3 and update some classnames with `-bs-` it still shows the 'XSS' alert:
https://codepen.io/bmthomas/pen/YzoQBdM

I'm having a hard time viewing these CVE's as XSS, the actual issue seems to be *"preventDefault not called on links with `data-(bs-)slide` with malformed href attributes"*. Calling this XSS by assuming the href attribute has already been compromised seems a bit far fetched and would be an issue even without bootstrap. Bootstrap doesn't actually evaluate the href, it's just not preventing you from click-activating it. But maybe I'm missing something crucial since there are no public patches for this issue.

author: julien-deramond

body: I don't mean to necro bump the discussion but this seems to be the most appropriate location to share my research into this CVE. This has been bugging me for quite a while now as it has been appearing in the software composition analysis (NexusIQ) scans. The existing Bootstrap source code (v3/v4/v5) already checks if the value in the "href" or "data-target" ("data-bs-target" for v5) attribute points to a target with the carousel class. And if so, blocks the click event from propagating further. And if not, returns the event back to the browser. Reviewing the PoC code as seen at https://www.herodevs.com/vulnerability-directory/cve-2024-6484?bootstrap-nes It is not setup correctly to either use href="#myCarousel" or data-target="#myCarousel" to point to an element with the carousel class. If you add data-target="#myCarousel" to the PoC at https://codepen.io/Trey-McCallie/pen/NWZKERe as follows, then the XSS won't be triggered and the carousel moves to the previous slide as expected ``` Previous (XSS) ``` Blocking all the `` tags that has the "data-slide" or "data-slide-to" attribute isn't a good idea as we cannot be entirely sure the attributes will be exclusively written for use by Bootstrap. This could cause legitimate click actions on elements with 'data-slide' or 'data-slide-to' attributes that are not meant for bootstrap carousel to be dropped and not processed by the browser. By returning the legitimate click actions back to the browser to process seems to be the most graceful approach to not break anything. Why is this an issue in the first place?

body:
Hi @marksmall 

I’m not entirely sure I grasped the full architecture, but here's a possible idea to explore.

I assume that at some point `bootstrap.css` gets loaded, and our reboot rules apply across the entire app, potentially causing conflicts. For example, the following rule would affect all `ol, ul` elements globally:

https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/dcec1df61f107e2b449486aafefbb1d14abe0333/dist/css/bootstrap.css#L288-L291

Depending on how Bootstrap is being loaded, you might be able to encapsulate all the Bootstrap CSS within a `.fake-class` Then, you would apply this class only to the part of the app where Bootstrap is being used. This way, any other parts of the app that aren't supposed to use Bootstrap would be unaffected by styles like the `ol, ul` rule, since they wouldn't have the `.fake-class` in the DOM.

However, this approach would only work if the Bootstrap section is isolated within the app and isn't the main framework in use. And also, if Bootstrap is not loaded with the CDN; so [built via Sass like this](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/customize/sass/#importing) (or downloaded manually).

Another weird idea—though a bit unconventional—if Bootstrap is the primary framework and there are other non-Bootstrap components embedded in the page: Instead of using a `.fake-class`, you could try wrapping the entire Bootstrap CSS with something like `:not(.exclude-bootstrap)`. This way, wherever you want to prevent Bootstrap's default styles from applying, you could add the `.exclude-bootstrap` class.

I haven't tested obviously these things. Even if they can't work or are dumb (sorry for that), I hope they could lead you to some new ideas to resolve your problem :)
author: julien-deramond

body:
Hello @sonvnn 

There's no specific folder for RTL. We use the same SCSS files with `/* rtl:* */` "commands" in them; see https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/fecd219983539aa0110a7e09d31b8aa4d5fb4348/scss/_popover.scss#L78-L102

The generated CSS file is, however, different, and available at https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.3.3/dist/css/bootstrap.rtl.css

body:
Bootstrap is a CSS framework, used to create responsive applications. It provides a collection of pre-designed HTML, CSS, and JavaScript components, such as grids, navigation bars, buttons, forms and more, which help developers build user interfaces quickly and efficiently.

You can think of it like a container, a large box which you put in your project to help you achieve your design with less CSS code.

To better understand Bootstrap, I would suggest taking a look at the [official documentation](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/getting-started/introduction/), or watch Youtube tutorials.

The easiest way is to include the [cdn links](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/getting-started/introduction/#cdn-links) in your main html file. 
With this simple step, you will be able to use Bootstrap in your project. To test it, you can create a paragraph element and write the following css class: text-decoration-underline

This will have the following result: 
![result](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/4949f663-34e4-441e-b08a-981fdca4d727)
![class](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/13735932-52f2-4d64-b6f8-6aaa8649c00e)

Keep in mind that this is just the tip of the iceberg when it comes to the capabilities of Bootstrap. 
I hope this gave you enough to get you started, and motivation to continue your research!

body:
Bootstrap is a framework that helps you easily create websites. It is composed of HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, and provides various design templates.

Common web elements like buttons, navigation bars, and cards are pre-made, so you can easily add them.

To put it simply so that non-experts can easily understand, it is a method of using templates that have pre-made CSS and JavaScript interactions for frequently used web design elements such as buttons, navigation bars, and cards, so you can easily add quality design elements by finding the elements you want through the examples provided by Bootstrap and simply copying and pasting them.

Many experts also use it because it reduces the amount of coding required by customizing pre-made elements a little, thus shortening the work time.

Hi @ArtJustine ,  

Bootstrap is a popular front-end framework that helps you design responsive and mobile-first websites quickly. It provides pre-designed components like buttons, forms, modals, and grids that you can easily customize using HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.  

To implement Bootstrap while learning, follow these steps:  
1. **Include Bootstrap**: Add Bootstrap to your project via CDN or download it from the Bootstrap website.  
2. **Use Bootstrap Components**: Start using Bootstrap classes in your HTML to create layouts and components.  
3. **Customize with CSS**: Override Bootstrap styles or add your own custom styles for a unique look.  
4. **Learn the Grid System**: Familiarize yourself with the Bootstrap grid system to create responsive layouts.  
5. **Utilize JavaScript Components**: Explore Bootstrap’s JavaScript plugins for interactive components.  

Thanks!

I think the classes for padding and margin `(p-* and m-*)` are not intended for images. In your case, it is probably the interaction with `img-fluid` that causes the effect.

body:
I wasn’t part of the core team when it was initially implemented, but from my perspective, it serves as a security measure that gets checked for every PR. This not only helps prevent mistakes but also acts, for me personally, as a reminder that every addition comes with a cost and should be minimized, especially for eco-design reasons.

> And if the file needs to get bigger in the future, what’s the best way to handle that?

If the file needs to grow, it should be a considered decision, but it’s not an issue. We can modify the limit in the same PR that updates or adds content. The rule is to increase the value by increments of 0.25.
author: julien-deramond

body:
I'm not sure if there are explicit rules documented somewhere, but it’s primarily tied to the spacing scale you're using. Common values like `0.25rem`, `0.75rem`, and `1.25rem` are based on a `16px` font size base, so, for instance, `0.25 * 16` gives you `4px`. If you use something like `0.19rem`, that would equal `3.04px`, which isn’t ideal because non-integer pixel values can lead to inconsistencies or rounding issues in rendering. 
On top of that, the choice between using `rem`, `px`, or `em` can also depend on how you want the layout to behave when users zoom in or out. It depends on the use cases and renderings.
author: julien-deramond

body:
Using `@extend` is more efficient for the bundled file size as it avoids code duplication by applying the same style to multiple selectors. However, since `@extend` cannot use dynamic values, it is recommended to switch to `@mixin` only when you need dynamic values.

`@mixin` is used for creating styles that require additional features like conditionals, loops, or user overrides, while `@extend` is used for simply copying existing styles.

Therefore, it is best to use them according to the situation.

Here's a quick checklist with code suggestions: 1. **Viewport Meta Tag**: Ensure your viewport tag is correctly set. Yours looks good: ```html ``` 2. **Flexbox and Grid CSS**: Double-check the Bootstrap classes used for layout. Sometimes, Safari has issues with flex properties. Here’s a simplified structure: ```html
...
...
...
``` 3. **CSS Reset**: Sometimes a CSS reset helps: ```css * { margin: 0; padding: 0; box-sizing: border-box; } ``` 4. **Inspect Element**: Use Safari’s Developer Tools (Right-click > Inspect Element) to check for any CSS conflicts. 5. **Update Safari**: Ensure that you're using the latest version of Safari as compatibility can vary. If issues persist, consider sharing the specific parts of your CSS that might be causing layout problems. Thanks!

Based on the code here:

https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/30e01525670386c23f9bc0d9752a3f8f70af73cc/js/src/dropdown.js#L312-L319

It seems the answer is no. The algorithm explicitly checks if the dropdown is inside a navbar and then enforces the attribute `data-bs-popper="static"`.

There may be a potential workaround, though: the algorithm determines whether it's in a navbar by checking for the presence of the `.navbar` class. If you create a class that duplicates  `.navbar` but uses a different class name, it might bypass the check. Otherwise, overriding this behavior directly doesn't seem possible.

body:
> because in this case i can just use Bootstrap class .dropdown-menu-end dropdown-menu-md-start right?

Yes, you can certainly do that based on your rendering needs. In our documentation, we consistently use `.dropdown-menu.dropdown-menu-end` for the theme selector, since the dropdown in the header is aligned to the right. This helps prevent any alignment issues across various breakpoints. Alternatively, we could have taken a more granular approach, such as using `dropdown-menu dropdown-menu-end dropdown-menu-xxl-start` (by testing that it actually works at any breakpoints).

body:
Hi @TechBright ,  

After upgrading Bootstrap from 4.5.3 to 5.3.3, the SCSS files you've mentioned are no longer available because Bootstrap 5 has made some changes, including removing or renaming certain components. Here are the alternatives you can use:

1. **Print**: Instead of `@import "../../../node_modules/bootstrap/scss/print";`, you can use Bootstrap's built-in print utility classes in your HTML and customize your print styles using CSS.

2. **Media**: The media utilities have been integrated into the core Bootstrap CSS, so you can use the responsive utility classes directly in your HTML.

3. **Jumbotron**: The jumbotron has been replaced with the `hero` component in Bootstrap 5. You can use the `.hero` class instead.

4. **Custom Forms**: Bootstrap 5 has restructured form components, so you should replace `@import "../../../node_modules/bootstrap/scss/custom-forms";` with the appropriate form classes from the new documentation.

5. **Code**: For code styling, use the new syntax highlighting classes provided by Bootstrap, like `.bg-dark` and `.text-white` for code blocks.

Make sure to check the [Bootstrap 5 migration guide](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/migration/) for detailed changes and how to adapt your styles accordingly.  

Thanks!

I'm not entirely sure, but it looks like there might be an incompatibility between the Sass version you're using and how Nuxt 3 handles module imports. Here are a few things I'd suggest trying:
1. Update Sass version: Try upgrading your Sass dependency to a more recent version, such as `1.78.0` (current version used by Bootstrap)
2. Adjust the import paths: If upgrading Sass isn’t possible, try modifying the import paths. Instead of `@import "bootstrap/scss/functions";`, use something like `@import "@/node_modules/bootstrap/scss/functions"`; to specify the full path more explicitly.
3. Share a reproducible environment: If neither of these steps work, could you try creating a reproducible environment in a tool like StackBlitz? This can help identify the root cause more accurately and allow for targeted troubleshooting.

Hope these steps could help to narrow down the issue!

author: JustSxm

body:
Our `.g*-*` (e.g. `.gx-5`) utility presented at https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/layout/gutters/ works by defining a gutter size which is used by the row margin right/left (calculated), but also for the padding left/right of the column:

> Gutters are the gaps between column content, created by horizontal padding. We set padding-right and padding-left on each column, and use negative margin to offset that at the start and end of each row to align content.

![Screenshot 2024-11-15 at 19 09 10](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d8e4bc88-29c3-48eb-bad9-68adb7a82b2e)

If you replace `.gx-5` with `.gap-4` for instance, it's no more calculated values by Bootstrap by playing with margins and paddings, it's the real gap system from flexbox:

![Screenshot 2024-11-15 at 19 10 20](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ddd9cd2a-99fd-4a9f-bf72-1c414c59d201)

So slightly different, but not that far neither.

body:
This is not Bootstrap problem. Bootstrap works. 

**Your CSS is changed from the default Bootstrap CSS and it changes specificity which makes it a different CSS.**

# Your case
1. `.row[ngcontent--ng--c3120S72962] > *[ngcontent-ng-c3120372962]`
   - Specificity: (0,3,0)
     - 1 class selector (`.row`)
     - 2 attribute selectors (2×`[ngcontent--ng--c3120S72962]`)
2. `.col-md-4[ngcontent--ng--c3120S72962]`
   - Specificity: (0,2,0)
     - 1 class selector (`.col-md-4`)
     - 1 attribute selector (`[ngcontent--ng--c3120S72962]`)

So 
- Attribute selector has the same specificity as class selector.
- The first selector has higher specificity due to the additional attribute selector.
- It takes precedence when setting properties regardless of order.

# Bootstrap
1. `.row > *`
   - Specificity: (0,1,0)
     - 1 class selector (`.row`)

2. `.col-md-4`
   - Specificity: (0,1,0)
     - 1 class selector (`.col-md-4`)

So
- These selectors have equal specificity
- The later selector in the stylesheet (`.col-md-4`) wins if both set the same property like width.


# Solution
Do not change Bootstrap's CSS.

body:
I believe migrating it without some changes in the source code is not possible. I began the migration, but the first obstacle I encountered was that `_variables.scss` required certain functions from `_functions.scss`. Additionally, some functions from `_functions.scss`, like `color-contrast`, require specific variables, so some functions need to be rewritten. Another issue is that mixins with an underscore, such as `_assert-ascending`, do not work.

I’m not sure if you will permit the following changes in v5, but if you do, I can help you because I need to implement this migration in my Bootstrap-based project https://github.com/coreui/coreui due to the numerous support requests I've received about this Sass warning.

body:
Hello @journpy 

I've converted your issue into a discussion since the toggle buttons seem to work as expected, as shown in the documentation.
If you already have `popper.min.js` and `bootstrap.min.js` scripts properly included in your project, the functionality should work out of the box.

Based on your description, it’s likely an issue with your project’s configuration or setup, or perhaps an incompatibility with Django Bootstrap. I noticed you’ve already opened https://github.com/zostera/django-bootstrap5/issues/735 in their repository, which is a great step since I haven’t personally worked with Django Bootstrap.

Here are a few suggestions to help debug the problem:
- Test whether other `data-bs-*` attributes work as expected (instantiate an accordion for instance). This can help determine if the issue is with Django Bootstrap’s handling of the global `data-bs-*` system or specifically with the toggle buttons.
- Use your browser’s developer tools to check the console for any warning or error messages that might give clues about what’s going wrong.

Maybe you could try to create a reproducible project for folks to help with [StackBlitz](https://stackblitz.com/) for example.

body: To determine the optimal bundle size for your project or library, analyze your current bundle using tools like Webpack Bundle Analyzer or Source Map Explorer. These tools help you visualize what's included and identify large dependencies or unused code. Considering factors like the target audience's device capabilities and network conditions, smaller bundles are better for mobile users.
author: jonhubby

body: To determine your project's bundle size, use bundler-specific tools like **webpack-bundle-analyzer** (Webpack) or **rollup-plugin-visualizer** (Rollup) to generate visual reports, or **source-map-explorer** for generic analysis. Check compressed sizes via CLI with `gzip-size` and monitor dependencies with **Bundlephobia**. Enforce size limits using **Webpack performance budgets** or **size-limit**, and integrate checks into CI/CD pipelines. Always analyze production builds (minified/compressed) and account for all code-split chunks to identify optimization opportunities.

body:
Hey!

The Bootstrap spacing scale (`$spacers`) is globally defined in the SCSS variables file, ensuring consistent behavior across all breakpoints.

Just to clarify your requirement, are you looking to modify only the spacing utilities (like `.m-*` or `.p-*`) to behave differently between mobile and desktop? Or are you aiming to apply this change across the entire codebase, including all components that rely on spacers (e.g., `.card`, `.container`, etc.)?

If you're focusing only on utilities, you can leverage the [Utilities API](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/utilities/api/) alongside Sass overrides to achieve this. However, if your goal is to include components as well, the task becomes more complex. You'd likely need to integrate custom properties in multiple places—some of which might not currently exist or are inconsistently implemented across Bootstrap components.

I haven't looked into the details of how to achieve that, but this is my initial impression based on your question.

```

End aligned text on viewports sized SM (small) or wider.

End aligned text on viewports sized MD (medium) or wider.

End aligned text on viewports sized LG (large) or wider.

End aligned text on viewports sized XL (extra large) or wider.

End aligned text on viewports sized XXL (extra extra large) or wider.

author: enzedonline

body:
In newspapers and other printed media, the font size or column width cannot be changed after printing.

In addition, no trained typesetter leaves the result of justification to the automatic setting (in InDesign or Quark Express etc.). Kerning is an art that has to be learned. Browsers do not master this art.

body:
Hi there,

Just to clarify, this is my personal perspective and not an official statement on behalf of the rest of the core team.

The delay in releasing version 5.3.4 isn't about being overly ambitious but rather about syncing the availability of the core team for the release process, along with making decisions for the next minor and major versions. For example, I've continued merging patches into v5.3.4 since it hasn't been released yet, but it could have been released earlier as-is, with additional patches handled in subsequent v5.3.x releases. In my opinion, it's more a matter of balancing personal commitments and the time available to contribute to OSS and Bootstrap. The core team is relatively small, and this project has a large volume of issues, discussions, and interactions to manage.

I'm also working on simplifying the release process to make it easier to release updates more frequently in the future and avoid situations like the one we're experiencing with v5.3.4.

Regarding the deprecation warnings, these can't be addressed in a patch release because fixing them requires a significant refactor of Bootstrap's codebase. You can follow the ongoing work on this here: https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/41112.

Hope this helps clarify things :)

body:
Based on your [Bootstrap and Webpack Repository](https://github.com/RockyMdee32/Bootstrap-and-Webpack):
- You modified `node_modules` so you have to `rm -rf node_modules` first
- Then you can `npm i` to install the regular packages
- In `styles.scss` you need to `@import "bootstrap/scss/bootstrap";` instead of using `@use`
- Then, if you run `npm run build` and render the `index.html` in your browser, it works well

When you run `npm run start` the deprecation warnings from Sass can appear in the browser. In that case, you'll need to check how to ignore/silent these warnings via webpack.

body: It sounds like the issue might be related to how Vite processes and serves assets compared to using Bootstrap via a link. When using Vite, the way static assets like images are handled might differ, and it's possible that Vite's dev server is either not serving the images correctly or they're being incorrectly referenced. Bootstrap itself won't affect image visibility by default, nor does it alter how images should be displayed. The issue seems to be more about how Vite handles your asset paths and MIME types.

body:
I'm far from being a NuGet expert, but I think the key to deploying content files in NuGet packages is to include them in both `content` and `contentFiles` paths in your .nuspec file; something like:

```nuspec
<package>
  <metadata>
    <!-- ... other metadata ... -->
    <contentFiles>
      <files include="**/*" />
    </contentFiles>
  </metadata>
  <files>
    <!-- For traditional .NET Framework projects -->
    <file src="your\source\*.css" target="content\Content" />
    <file src="your\source\*.js" target="content\Scripts" />

    <!-- For modern SDK-style projects -->
    <file src="your\source\*.css" target="contentFiles\any\any\css" />
    <file src="your\source\*.js" target="contentFiles\any\any\js" />
  </files>
</package>
```

You can find the Bootstrap `.nuspec` files here: https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/tree/main/nuget.
Hope this helps!

body:
This will be achieved with the help of CSS, Bootstrap on it's own can't do it. 
Let's say you have two td's with class td1 and td2. 
In order for the two tds to be affected when a radio button (class name: radio-btn) is pressed, you will have to write the following CSS code:


`
.radio-btn:checked + label + table td.td1,
.radio-btn:checked + label + table td.td2 {
    background-color: #d4edda; /* Highlight both td1 and td2 */
}
`

This is better because it is using the class names

body:
There is no easy fix at the moment because the `ip` library hasn't had an official release with the patch yet, even though it's highly requested by the community ([see discussion here](https://github.com/indutny/node-ip/issues/150)). Once a new version is available, Bootstrap will automatically pick up the update when dependencies are refreshed.

That said, there's **no real cause for concern** - this package is only used in Bootstrap's **development** and is listed under `devDependencies`, meaning it has no impact on the final Bootstrap library. It is only used for running [Karma tests locally](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/0f13e1c2e7acb17fd17d49ef61ef33edc2285fd8/js/tests/karma.conf.js#L102), where it helps retrieve the machine's IP address for the test runner.

For now, we just need to wait for the official update, but this won't affect anyone using Bootstrap in production or working on their own forks.

body:
Hello @Nvn2708,
Currently, Bootstrap is not compatible with Sass modules. The warnings you're seeing are expected, as Bootstrap has not yet migrated to Sass modules. Work is in progress to address this, but it will take some time.
In the meantime, I recommend continuing to use Bootstrap with `@import` and either ignoring the deprecation messages or silencing them if they become too distracting.
Some references:
- https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues/29853
- https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues/40849
- https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues/40962

body:
The scope of Bootstrap v6 is still somewhat undefined. An initial exploration was started by Mark in https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/41236. Some features have been tentatively marked for v6 in the [v6 project board](https://github.com/orgs/twbs/projects/38) and within [open issues labeled "v6"](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues?q=is%3Aissue+state%3Aopen+label%3Av6).

We're also considering whether to release major versions more frequently. This could mean reducing the scope of v6 in favor of shipping a leaner release sooner, followed by a v7 on a shorter timeline—rather than waiting another 3–4 years.

We specifically worked with version 1.77.7 in https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/40623, but versions 1.77.8 and 1.78.0 should also be compatible. Compatibility breaks starting from 1.79.0 onward.

Just curious—why were you considering changing it to 1.77.6? Did you encounter any issues with 1.77.7?
author: julien-deramond

body:
> Did you encounter any issues with 1.77.7?

Not since Bootstrap 5.3.4 : https://github.com/BesLogic/releaf-canopeum/pull/359

I just found it odd to recommend pinning to `sass@1.77.7` if
- `sass@1.77.6` is the latest no issue with `bootstrap@5.3.3`
- and `sass@1.78` is the latest no issue with `bootstrap@5.3.4`

So it felt like a typo. It's just me misunderstanding the section "If You're Still Seeing Warnings..." as if it meant "if you're still seeing warning **because you can't bump bootstrap**"
Rather than "if you're still seeing warning **with `sass@1.78` because we fixed specifically for `sass@1.77.7`**"
author: SamuelT-Beslogic

body: Thanks for the feedback! I understand how the message might have been confusing. I originally wrote it after we merged the fix for version 1.77.7, but 1.78.0 was released around the same time without adding new deprecated warnings. I'll update the message to reference 1.78.0 instead of 1.77.7—it should make things clearer. Hopefully that helps! 😊
author: julien-deramond

body:
Just some thoughts, I hope not too antagonistic...

> 🧨 Sass 1.79.x Introduced a Breaking Change

Does it though? It is deprecated, not removed, specifically because removing it would be a breaking change.
So it depends on when v1.x will be EOL.

EDIT: Normally a deprecation should provide a replacement that is available in the same version.
Then the calling code would do a conditional call to one or the other. Or it could use a polyfill.
Not sure if that is an option here. I have to look at that specific issue.

> This will be a breaking change for Sass users, but it’s necessary for long-term compatibility.

> Why not call this v6? Because it doesn’t change how Bootstrap is used, only how it’s compiled. We think v6 should represent a bigger conceptual shift. Like a new epoch.

If by "use" we just mean writing html templates with bootstrap classes, then sure.
But it is quite common for website projects or add-on packages to use the sass features of Bootstrap..
I do see myself as a "user" when I write sass files with bootstrap.

Personally, I see nothing wrong with spamming major versions.
Having a v6 just for these breaking changes in sass would mean that we can continue to do minor releases like 5.4.0, 5.5.0 etc, while at the same time releasing 6.0.0, 6.1.0 etc.
Of course there can be a question of whether you (the team) would want to invest the time to work on both 5.x and 6.x, and for how long.

Then v7 can be the big ambitious rewrite.
Though in some cases I might even prefer seeing a new package name instead of an "epic" rewrite on the same old name, depending how severe the changes are.

-----

Now from the "Epoch Semantic Versioning" article.

> I see versioning as a way to communicate changes to users

I see this as fundamentally missing the point.
Versioning is a way to communicate compatibility with other packages.
A package can declare a dependency to bootstrap as "^5.3", because it assumes that any future version from the 5.x version range will be backwards compatible.
The disruption is less to the user but to the ecosystem of packages depending on bootstrap.

EDIT: This said, the final conclusion from that blog post is still aligned with what I suggest here: Don't be afraid to do a major for "breaking stuff without flashy new features", and then a bigger major for "ambitous new features".

author: J4KU8Dev

body:
Hi, and welcome!

Editing plain CSS still works with Bootstrap, and for many use cases, it's totally fine to stick with that approach.

Sass (and similar tools) help streamline larger projects by adding features like variables, nesting, and modularization. In Bootstrap's case, it makes it easier to customize things like colors or spacing globally and recompile the CSS to fit your needs. Bootstrap is designed with this kind of customization in mind. Using Sass also helps keep your customizations more maintainable, especially as the framework evolves and its precompiled CSS may change between versions.

That said, if you're comfortable editing the compiled CSS directly and it works for your project, that's completely valid, there's no really wrong way to use Bootstrap :)
author: julien-deramond

body:
Hi Danielx64,

Welcome to the community! It’s understandable to feel a bit overwhelmed with all the new tools and terminology out there. Your experience with plain CSS is still very relevant, and many developers share your preference for simplicity.

Sass does offer some nice features for larger projects, like variables and nesting, which can save time when making global changes. However, if you’re comfortable with your current workflow and it meets your needs, there’s no need to rush into learning new tools. Stick with what works for you, and you can always explore Sass later if you find a project that could benefit from it. Everyone has their approach, and that’s what makes web development so diverse!

body:
You've actually already given yourself the answer to your question:

> This issue persist when modal container is put inside fixed position container like below code

This behavior is also described in the [documentation](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/components/modal/). How it works, point 4.

> Modals use position: fixed, which can sometimes be a bit particular about its rendering. Whenever possible, place your modal HTML in a top-level position to avoid potential interference from other elements. You’ll likely run into issues when nesting a .modal within another fixed element.

I always do it like this:

Place the `<button type="button" class="btn btn-primary" data-bs-toggle="modal" ...` wherever you want but the `<div class="modal fade" ...` at the end, just before `</body>`.
author: patkon

body: Thanks for the suggestion! I hadn’t considered placing the modal outside the fixed container. I’ll give that a try and see if it resolves the issue. It's good to know that modals have this behavior when nested in fixed elements, and I’ll keep that in mind for future implementations.
author: jonhubby

body:
That makes sense now 👍 — I ran into the same backdrop issue recently. Moving the modal outside of the fixed container solved it for me too, and it also kept the backdrop behavior consistent across different browsers. Definitely a good reminder that Bootstrap modals prefer to live at the top level.

By the way, if anyone’s working on UI/UX projects where light control or ambient distractions matter (especially for testing dark/light modes), investing in proper window coverings Orlando FL style solutions can actually make your workspace much more reliable for design work.

author: MargateSteve

body:
Hugo is great and I'm a fan and still use it for many projects. If it works for you and you're happy with it continue doing so! Astro is neat, but there's a few things I don't like about it (such as lack of HTML minification options and slower build times).

Astro is only used (in this project) for the Bootstrap docs and I don't think that means they are recommending or suggesting that it should be used with that particular static site generator/build tool. Bootstrap is framework agnostic and it's best that way :-) 

You can read about the reasons for the migration in @julien-deramond's PR here https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/38319

body:
The `integrity` attribute enables Subresource Integrity (SRI)—it verifies that a fetched JS or CSS file hasn't been tampered with by checking its cryptographic hash. This is not the main topic, but there is some information about why `integrity` is important (and how to generate it) when using Bootstrap at [Download > Alternative CDNs](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/getting-started/download/#alternative-cdns).

`crossorigin="anonymous"` is needed when loading from a CDN so the browser can fetch the file and still verify its integrity.

They're not required, but strongly recommended for third-party resources to prevent supply chain attacks.
author: julien-deramond

body: Thanks Julien. I'm only surprised that Bootstrap itself does not do more to promote integrity and crossorigin attribution so I will certainly look at your download > alternative cdn link.
author: quivis

body: Just to add to what Julien said, if you're using a CDN like jsDelivr or CDNJS, they usually provide the integrity attribute with the link. For self-hosted files, you can generate the hash using a tool like [SRI Hash Generator](https://www.srihash.org/). Keep in mind that even small changes to the file will require a new hash. Using integrity with crossorigin="anonymous" is a solid way to protect against unexpected changes or supply chain attacks.

body:
The grid system can feel tricky at first, but here are a few tips that helped me:

1. **Start with the Basics** – Use a `.container` > `.row` > `.col-*` structure. Stick to this nesting to avoid layout issues.
2. **Understand Breakpoints** – Use classes like `.col-sm-6`, `.col-md-4`, etc., to control layouts across screen sizes. Test with browser dev tools.
3. **Use Spacing Utilities** – Instead of custom CSS, use Bootstrap’s margin/padding classes like `mb-3`, `px-4` for consistent spacing.
4. **Practice** – Tinker with examples from the Bootstrap docs: https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/layout/grid/
5. **Watch Nesting** – Columns must be direct children of rows. Misnesting is a common cause of misalignment.

Once you get the hang of it, building responsive layouts becomes much easier.

body:
As you said, you're likely seeing performance issues because Bootstrap's global event listeners (from `bootstrap.min.js`) are processing clicks on your Phaser canvas, even though they don't affect it.

> Is there an official way to tell Bootstrap to ignore events on specific DOM elements?

I'm not the more knowledgeable in JS in the core team, but I don't think there's a Bootstrap API that allows opting-out elements from event delegation. Bootstrap doesn't "scope" its handlers.

> Can Bootstrap's event delegation be configured to exclude certain elements?

I'd say not directly. You could only monkey-patch or override event handlers.

> Are there known conflicts with Phaser?

Not known, but canvas-based libraries like Phaser surely don't expect global UI frameworks like Bootstrap to intercept events.

Here are other few things to try:

1. **Remove Bootstrap JS (`bootstrap.min.js`)**: If you're only using Bootstrap for styling and not for modals, tooltips, etc., just using `bootstrap.min.css` would avoid the problem entirely.
2. **Switch to a Vue implementation of Bootstrap** such as https://github.com/bootstrap-vue-next/bootstrap-vue-next. It's an early implementation of Vue 3, Bootstrap 5 and Typescript that may handle differently the events.
3. **Try to use [`stopImmediatePropagation()`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Event/stopImmediatePropagation)** instead of `stopPropagation()` in the tests you mentioned.
4. **Try to wrap the Phaser canvas in a Shadow DOM** (e.g., inside a custom Web Component). This could maybe isolate event propagation completely, preventing Bootstrap (or anything else in the main DOM) from seeing the events. It may require more setup to integrate with Vue + Phaser.
5. **If you don't find any technical workaround, switch to another Vue component library**.

body:
Hi @wernste, No worries at all — we all start somewhere! 😄 What you’re seeing is actually **expected behavior in Bootstrap 5**. Here’s why: 1. **Heading and Components Have Default Spacing** * Elements like `

`, `.card`, `.form-control`, and other Bootstrap components come with built-in padding or margin defined in Bootstrap’s CSS. * Normal text (like plain `

` or ``) **does not** have extra padding or margin by default — Bootstrap leaves it up to you to add spacing as needed using utility classes like `mb-3`, `p-2`, etc. 2. **Bootstrap Utility Classes Are Your Friend** * If you want consistent spacing for normal text, you can use Bootstrap spacing utilities: ```html

This is some text with bottom margin.

This paragraph has padding and a background.

``` * These utilities work on all elements and let you mimic the spacing you see on Bootstrap examples. 3. **Inspect the Examples Carefully** * Bootstrap examples often wrap elements in containers (`.container`, `.row`, `.col`) which also apply padding/margin. * Make sure your layout uses the same structure, or else spacing may look “off”. **In short:** Nothing is broken — headings and components just have default spacing, while plain text does not. Use Bootstrap spacing utilities to get consistent padding/margin wherever you need it.

body:
The bootstrap-rubygem often lags behind the latest Bootstrap releases, and since gems like bootstrap_form and bootstrap-email depend on it, you cannot import Bootstrap another way without breaking dependencies.

Options:
- Wait for the gem to be officially updated.
- Fork bootstrap-rubygem, replace its Bootstrap source with 5.3.8, build the gem locally, and use that in your project.
- Document your workaround and consider submitting a PR to the gem to help the community.

body:
Hi @giampieropace,

Thanks for the suggestion!

Having **responsive font-size utilities** sounds like a really useful addition, especially in today’s mobile-first world where controlling typography across different breakpoints is key for good UX.

Something like `text-lg-5` (where font size adjusts based on the screen size) would align nicely with existing spacing utilities (`p-md-4`, `m-sm-2`, etc.) and make it much easier to manage text responsiveness directly via utility classes, without writing custom CSS or media queries.

This could help speed up development and enforce consistency across projects. My only consideration would be ensuring the utility system stays scalable and doesn’t bloat with too many predefined sizes.

Overall, I’m fully in favor of this idea and would love to see it considered in future releases.

Looking forward to hearing what others think!

body:
> * is it possible to use different variants for native radio, checkbox?
> * I try `focus-ring focus-ring-secondary`, but when click outline become primary color

Hello [sysmat](https://github.com/sysmat),

That's a good question. You've correctly observed that while you can use focus-ring utilities, they don't always override the native browser's focus outline on default radio and checkbox inputs.

This happens because the native focus style is often managed directly by the browser and isn't fully controllable with Bootstrap's utility classes. These utilities are mainly designed to style the custom form components that Bootstrap provides, not the native ones.

To fix this, you'll need to use some custom CSS to target the :focus pseudo-class of the native elements and override the browser's default style.

You can add this CSS to your project to achieve the **secondary** color effect:

> `input[type="radio"]:focus,
> input[type="checkbox"]:focus {
>   outline: 0;
>   box-shadow: 0 0 0 .25rem rgba(var(--bs-secondary-rgb),.25);
> }`

_Hope this helps you with your project!_

body:
By design, the customClass option in Bootstrap is only evaluated when the tooltip is created/shown, not continuously while it’s visible. That’s why you’re seeing the “stale” state — the function isn’t re-invoked just because your trigger element’s classes changed after the tooltip was already open.

Why this happens

On mouseenter/focus, Bootstrap calls your customClass function and applies the result to the tooltip element.

Once rendered, the tooltip’s DOM is independent — it won’t automatically re-sync with changes to the trigger element unless you explicitly tell it to.

Options to keep it in sync

Force a refresh
If your trigger’s state changes while the tooltip is visible, you can call:

tooltipInstance.update();


or hide/show it again:

tooltipInstance.hide();
tooltipInstance.show();


That re-evaluates customClass().

Use a MutationObserver
Watch for class changes on the trigger and refresh the tooltip if needed:

const observer = new MutationObserver(() => tooltipInstance.update());
observer.observe(element, { attributes: true, attributeFilter: ['class'] });


Manual toggling
Instead of relying on customClass, toggle a CSS class directly on the tooltip DOM (.tooltip) in your own event handlers when the trigger changes.

author: MasaruKitajima

body:
What you’re seeing isn’t mystical Bootstrap corruption. It’s the boring reality of mixing two tooltip systems that absolutely **hate** coexisting: BS4 (jQuery-driven) and BS5 (vanilla JS). Once BS5 is introduced, even briefly, it can leave behind build artifacts, cached bundles, and duplicated CSS/JS that keep overriding your BS4 behavior long after you yank the code out.

Here’s the human-ish breakdown:

**1. You probably still have BS5 tooltip JS somewhere in your compiled output.**
Even after reverting, Angular’s build cache or node_modules leftovers can keep stale chunks lying around. BS5’s tooltip script runs, sees anything that looks like a tooltip target, and happily hijacks it.

This is why your validation tooltips no longer update. jQuery tries to modify the tooltip… BS5 already attached its own instance and just won’t let go.

Feels great, right?

**2. Bootstrap 4 and Bootstrap 5 tooltips cannot run at the same time unless you enjoy pain.**
They register different event handlers, initialize differently, store instances differently, and use different attributes. Whichever loads last usually “wins.”

Your app got stuck in a weird hybrid state.

**3. Reverting the commit does not revert the compiled output.**
Angular caches aggressively. The browser caches aggressively. Tooling caches aggressively. Your dev environment clings to old bundles like a raccoon guarding a trash can.

**4. The fix is stupidly simple but annoying:**
You need to *force* the environment to purge everything. Not code. Everything around the code.

Do this in order:

• Delete `node_modules`
• Delete `package-lock.json` (or your lockfile)
• Delete Angular cache:

```
ng cache clean
```

• Delete your build/dist folder
• Restart your dev server
• Hard-refresh your browser (or clear browser cache completely)

If you're doing SSR or have a CDN layer, purge those too. Cached bundles can be surprisingly persistent.

**5. Make sure no Bootstrap 5 scripts/CSS are lingering in your Angular build config.**
Check:
`angular.json`
`index.html`
your global styles
any CDN includes
any imported JS in your components
Webpack config overrides
lazy modules

Even one stray BS5 file is enough to bring the chaos back.

**6. BS4 tooltips won’t behave correctly again until BS5 is totally gone.**
Until then, jQuery will try to update a tooltip that BS5 already replaced, so the “stuck validation message” symptom keeps happening.

body: Hey Dzembak, # This is not controlled by Bootstrap Bootstrap provides CSS and JavaScript components, but it does not include product-category templates or generate image markup. The `loading` attribute is native HTML and must be added by the application, CMS, or theme rendering the `` element: Based on your mention of product categories, this appears to be related to WooCommerce or a WordPress theme rather than Bootstrap itself. # If this is WooCommerce WordPress normally calculates image-loading attributes automatically. It may intentionally omit `loading="lazy"` from images likely to appear above the fold, since lazy-loading the Largest Contentful Paint image can reduce performance. Check multiple products farther down the category page, not only the first image. If a custom theme bypasses that behavior, you can replace WooCommerce's product-loop thumbnail callback from a child theme or custom plugin: remove_action( 'woocommerce_before_shop_loop_item_title', 'woocommerce_template_loop_product_thumbnail', 10 ); add_action( 'woocommerce_before_shop_loop_item_title', function () { echo woocommerce_get_product_thumbnail( 'woocommerce_thumbnail', array( 'loading' => 'lazy', 'decoding' => 'async', ) ); }, 10 ); Do not modify WooCommerce or Bootstrap core files because an update will overwrite the change. I would also avoid forcing lazy loading on every image. The first visible product image may be better left eagerly loaded, while off-screen products should use `loading="lazy"`. To identify the exact template that needs changing, we would need the name and version of the WordPress theme or plugin producing the product grid, plus the rendered `` markup from DevTools. References: - https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/content/images/ - https://developer.wordpress.org/reference/functions/wp_get_attachment_image/ - https://developer.wordpress.org/reference/functions/wp_get_loading_optimization_attributes/ - https://woocommerce.github.io/code-reference/files/woocommerce-includes-wc-template-functions.html

author: julien-deramond

body:
Hey brother,

# Your interpretation is reasonable, but the documentation example is broken

Waiting for the modal's completed `hidden` event before calling `dispose()` is the correct approach. The example in the documentation contains several separate problems.

## 1. The event name is invalid

This:

    shown.bs.hidden

is not a Bootstrap event. It appears to combine parts of two different events.

The relevant modal events are:

- `shown.bs.modal`: the modal has finished opening
- `hidden.bs.modal`: the modal has finished closing

## 2. The methods belong to the Modal instance

`document.querySelector()` returns an `HTMLElement`, which does not normally have Bootstrap's `hide()` or `dispose()` methods.

You need both the element and its Bootstrap instance:

    const modalElement = document.querySelector('#myModal')
    const modal = bootstrap.Modal.getOrCreateInstance(modalElement)

## 3. The listener should be registered before calling `hide()`

A modal with the `.fade` class normally completes asynchronously, so registering the listener immediately after `hide()` may appear to work.

However, Bootstrap executes the callback immediately when no transition is involved. In that case, `hidden.bs.modal` could be emitted before the next line registers the listener.

The reliable version is:

    const modalElement = document.querySelector('#myModal')
    const modal = bootstrap.Modal.getOrCreateInstance(modalElement)

    modalElement.addEventListener('hidden.bs.modal', () => {
      modal.dispose()
    }, { once: true })

    modal.hide()

Using `{ once: true }` also prevents the cleanup callback from remaining attached if another Modal instance is later created on the same element.

# Why immediate disposal is unsafe

This would be unsafe:

    modal.hide()
    modal.dispose()

Internally, `hide()` queues work that still needs the Modal instance:

- finish the modal transition
- set `display: none`
- update ARIA attributes
- hide and remove the backdrop
- remove `modal-open` from the body
- restore scrollbar styles
- emit `hidden.bs.modal`

Meanwhile, `dispose()` removes the stored instance and sets its internal properties, including `_element`, to `null`.

When the pending transition callback eventually runs, it may attempt something equivalent to:

    this._element.style.display = 'none'

but `_element` is already `null`. This can produce an exception and leave behind a backdrop, locked body scrolling, or other incomplete cleanup.

That is the race condition the documentation is trying to warn about.

# Practical rule

- Use `hide()` when you only want to close the modal.
- Use `dispose()` when the instance is being permanently destroyed.
- If the modal is open, wait for `hidden.bs.modal` before disposing or removing its element.

If the modal is dynamically created, cleanup can be:

    modalElement.addEventListener('hidden.bs.modal', () => {
      modal.dispose()
      modalElement.remove()
    }, { once: true })

    modal.hide()

So yes, the idea you inferred is correct. The documentation should use `hidden.bs.modal`, operate on the actual Modal instance, and register the listener before `hide()`.

References:

- https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/components/modal/#events
- https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/v5.3.8/js/src/modal.js
- https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/v5.3.8/js/src/base-component.js
- https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues/41473
author: Lopesnextgen

body:
Bootstrap 5 uses a **nested `Map` structure** in its internal Data module (`src/dom/data.js`). Here's how it works:

```
Map<HTMLElement, Map<string, ComponentInstance>>
```

- The **outer Map** is keyed by DOM elements
- The **inner Map** is keyed by a component name string (e.g. `"bs.modal"`, `"bs.tooltip"`)
- Each element can only have **one instance per component type**

When you call `Modal.getInstance(element)`, it looks up the element in the outer Map, then retrieves the `"bs.modal"` entry from the inner Map. `getOrCreateInstance` does the same but creates a new instance if one doesn't exist.

### Garbage collection — the important part

**No, instances are NOT automatically garbage-collected when you remove the element from the DOM.** Since Bootstrap uses a regular `Map` (not `WeakMap`), it holds a strong reference to the element, which prevents the garbage collector from reclaiming either the element or the instance.

If you remove an element without calling `.dispose()` first, you'll leak memory. This matters in SPAs or any scenario where you're dynamically adding/removing elements. Always call `dispose()` before removing the element:

```js
const modal = bootstrap.Modal.getInstance(element);
if (modal) modal.dispose();
element.remove();
```

`dispose()` removes the entry from the data store, tears down event listeners, and makes both the element and instance eligible for GC.

**Why not WeakMap?** A `WeakMap` would allow automatic cleanup, but Bootstrap needs to iterate over stored instances in some cases (e.g., closing all open dropdowns), which `WeakMap` doesn't support since it's not enumerable.

body: @mdo as per https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues/42061 I'm opening a thread here instead. Here's my feedback, with a table representation, a visual representation, as well as live demo links: Table representation: **Current** | Variant | input-height | button-height | input-font-size | button-font-size | |------------|-------------|--------------|----------------|------------------| | lg | 53px | 44px | 17.5px | 16px | | md | 40px | 36px | 14px | 14px | | sm | 32px | 32px | 12.25px | 14px | **Proposed** | Variant | input-height | button-height | input-font-size | button-font-size | |-------------|-------------|--------------|----------------|------------------| | lg | 44px | 44px | 18px | 18px | | md | 38px | 38px | 16px | 16px | | sm | 32px | 32px | 14px | 14px | **Visual**: **Live**: Current version example at: https://codepen.io/pricop/pen/XJKvGob Proposed version example at: https://codepen.io/pricop/pen/LEZwvPL Personally, I feel like working with `1rem` set to `16px` is much easier to keep everything consistent, screens are getting larger every year, so sizing shouldn't be an issue. As a side note, I know these examples won't be for web, but could be used as a reference: **iOS** defaults to 17pt for body text, inputs, and buttons. Labels are 15pt (small). **Android** defaults to 16pt for body text, inputs, and buttons. Labels are 14pt in size (small). **macOS26** has enlarged pretty much all UI buttons inside app windows. I know BS is a web framework, and must cather to a plethora of web apps, including those with complex dashboards, but I've always felt that applying "small" is much easier to implement, than resetting the font-size to be larger, as it requires adjusting paddings and margins all over the place, and both BS4, and BS5 handled this very nicely. Going from `16px` on a component, to a `14px` on a component doesn't require many (if any at all) changes to margins/paddings. While going from `14px` to `16px` will require way more changes. And something to really consider, if you still want to go with the `14px` route for `-md` inputs & buttons: - Use `14px` for `-md` buttons and inputs, with larger padding that would equal a `36px` height for `-md` variants. - Keep the existing `-sm` variants as they are, only with an increased `14px` font size for the inputs. Feel free to mention me whenever you make a design decision on this, I'd be more than happy to offer feedback on it. 🙃
author: pricop

body:
If the decision is to go with `14px` for the regular components, my suggestion would be to make the `-md` input's height always match the button's height, here's how it would look like live: https://codepen.io/pricop/pen/gbwYOqe

Basically, both `-sm` and regular variants will share the same font-size, but the `-sm` variant will have smaller paddings (which is what BS6 already does for the `-sm` button anyways, so just the input's font-size will need adjustments).

body: i think v6 should give some more love to responsive `<table>`. tables are bread & butter in typical CRUD apps where Boostrap is frequently used. if table is wide (many columns) horizontal scrollbar is of course nice but experience could be improved on mobile devices - maybe something like "stacked table" where cells stack vertically on top of each other and column titles are pulled from `data-` attributes.
author: kminek

body:
I think one quality of life improvement would be a “sticky” column: where if you have a column that’s acting as headers, and you horizontally scrolling the table body, then that headers column stays static.

Think league tables in sports where the first column is usually the team name, and you’re scrolling statistics such as games played, goals, goal difference, points, etc. Those statistics then lose context if you can no longer see which team they’re related to.

Example: go to https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/football/tables, shrink your browser’s width, and then horizontally scroll the league table.

body:
v6 docs problems:
1. Page https://v6-dev--twbs-bootstrap.netlify.app/docs/6.0/examples/  [Dialogs] dialogs@2x.png or dialogs.png - links to image is giving 404. so card is broken 
2. Datepicker: floating calendar not hidden on mobile (390px)

**Page:** https://v6-dev--twbs-bootstrap.netlify.app/docs/6.0/forms/datepicker at Mobile view on iPhone for example 

### What fails

| Section | ID | Blocked by |
|---------|-----|-----------|
| **Date range** | `#datepicker4` | Stuck popup from **Multiple months** (`#datepickerMultiMonth`) |
| **First day of week** | `#datepicker6` | Stuck popup from **Multi-month date range** (`#datepickerRangeTwoMonths`) |

### Bug

Closing a datepicker sets `data-vc-calendar-hidden` on the floating `.vc` element but it stays `display: flex` at full height. Popups accumulate in `<body>` on every open/close.

Multi-month popups are 538–572px tall on a 390px screen — they cover the next input below.

### Reproduce

1. Open datepicker page at 390px width
2. Click **Multiple months** (`#datepickerMultiMonth`)
3. Press Escape — popup stays visible (572px tall, `display: flex`)
4. Try clicking **Date range** (`#datepicker4`) — blocked by the stuck popup

### Root cause

Missing CSS rule:

```css
.vc[data-vc-calendar-hidden] { display: none; }
```

The `.hide()` JS API has the same issue — sets the attribute, element stays rendered.

Works fine on desktop — popups don't overlap next inputs at wider viewports.

body:
:root {
  --bs-primary: oklch(62% 0.25 29);
}

will affect:

✅ .bg-primary
✅ .text-primary
✅ .btn-primary
✅ Hover state
✅ Active state
✅ Subtle background
✅ Borders
✅ Outline buttons

All automatically. ? , if this is the case , this will be huge .

Yes, if you update the root `--bs-primary-*` tokens, anything consuming that variable will be updated. So setting `--bs-primary-bg` to `--bs-orange-500` would change from blue to orange for buttons and more. You'd want to update all those theme tokens at once, either in our Sass map or manually in your custom CSS. More info is at https://v6-dev--twbs-bootstrap.netlify.app/docs/6.0/customize/theme/.

body:
Quick update to everyone here—I've started to address much of the feedback here. Because GH Discussions are a little limited, I've also made a new discussions section called v6 Feedback, where this thread now lives. This way we can all make new threads instead of piling on replies here. See https://github.com/orgs/twbs/discussions/categories/v6-feedback.

Since this thread opened, I've updated a lot of documentation (especially on theming and Sass), fixed some docs design issues, restored 16px base font size, and shipped a brand new method of customizing Bootstrap with a CSS variable based Sass token map. [Check out the Sass docs page for more info.](https://v6-dev--twbs-bootstrap.netlify.app/docs/6.0/customize/sass/#component-tokens)
