author: pricop

body:
We don't have to add extra size properties, just to rename the properties that are currently getting in the way of expanding the `$sizes` scale, which in this case are the `percentage` ones.

I've done this in **Bootstrap 6** and the CSS is almost identical 🥳.

**Before**: 275 KB (282,257 bytes)
**After**: 275 KB (282,374 bytes)

Just 117 **bytes** in difference.

`w-25` becomes `w-25/100`
`viewport-width-100` becomes `w-100vw`

We can also now have `w-10` for `10rem` and `w-10/100` for `10%`, no more naming conflicts 🚀.

Functionally, it gained two new extra classes: `max-w-100vw` and `max-h-100vh` (which can be removed if not needed).

The `viewport-width` was merged into `w`, `min-viewport-width` was merged into `min-w`, `viewport-height` was merged into `h`, `min-viewport-height` was merged into `min-h`. I see no reason for why these should have been separated in the first place, they both alter the same property.

And for those who need more, the map already provides hints to how it can be expanded, for example one could implement other size units, such as `dvh`, `lvh`, `svh`, etc., similarly to how `h-100vh` was implemented, same thing for percentage & rem units...

```
// Sizing utilities
// scss-docs-start utils-width
"width": (
  property: width,
  class: w,
  values: map.merge(
	$sizes,
	(
	  25\/100: 25%,
	  50\/100: 50%,
	  75\/100: 75%,
	  100\/100: 100%,
	  auto: auto,
	  100vw: 100vw,
	  min: min-content,
	  max: max-content,
	  fit: fit-content,
	)
  )
),
"max-width": (
  property: max-width,
  class: max-w,
  values: (
	100\/100: 100%,
	100vw: 100vw,
  )
),
"min-width": (
  property: min-width,
  class: min-w,
  values: (
	0: 0,
	100\/100: 100%,
	100vw: 100vw,
  )
),
// scss-docs-end utils-width
// scss-docs-start utils-height
"height": (
  property: height,
  class: h,
  values: (
	25\/100: 25%,
	50\/100: 50%,
	75\/100: 75%,
	100\/100: 100%,
	100vh: 100vh,
	auto: auto,
	min: min-content,
	max: max-content,
	fit: fit-content,
  )
),
"max-height": (
  property: max-height,
  class: max-h,
  values: (
	100\/100: 100%,
	100vh: 100vh,
  )
),
"min-height": (
  property: min-height,
  class: min-h,
  values: (
	0: 0,
	100\/100: 100%,
	100vh: 100vh,
  ),
),
// scss-docs-end utils-height
```
author: pricop

body:
I've noticed that if I set the class names to "h-10/100", the docs won't automatically update to display the style="" value, it would probably require a change in the way the class names and their values are parsed.

Perhaps the (better?) alternative would be using `h-10p`.

An advantage that I see to using `p` is that it's very semantic, and would make a bit more sense since we'd also have `vh` for `viewport-height` and `vw` for `viewport-width`, so it would be the value, followed by the unit.

| Class | Styles |
| ----------- | ----------- |
| h-100      | height: 100rem; |
| h-100p   | height: 100%; |
| h-100vh | height: 100vh; |

and for those who need more, they can always expand it following the same patteern, for example for this can be easily expanded to accomodate for `dynamic viewport`, `large viewport`, `small viewport`, one could have:

| Class | Styles |
| ----------- | ----------- |
| h-100dvh      | height: 100dvh; |
| h-100svh   | height: 100svh; |
| h-100lvh | height: 100lvh; |
| h-20p | height: 20%; |
| h-20 | height: 20rem; |
author: pricop

body:
Your utility doesn't just blur, it also applies a strong **saturate()** filter, which feels very opinionated (it increases the colors underneath quite a lot, and could hurt accessibility), so that particular bit would have to disappear if this is to be introduced, as some of us have generated additional filter utilities, for example `bd-filter-saturate`, `bd-filter-invert`, `bd-filter-grayscale`, etc.

I'd love to see backdrop blur being added out of the box, but the main problem that I currently see is that the CSS size of Bootstrap v6 currently sits at **275kb~**, it has almost doubled from **144kb~** in v4 (90%~ increase), which is pretty significant, and increased by about **45kb** from v5 (20%~ increase).

Loading times might actually become a problem if Bootstrap's CSS continues to grow in size, as most of the people will just hotload Boostrap from a CDN URL.

Perhaps there's a good way to shave off some CSS from other parts to make room for new utilities.

Or maybe this is the perfect case of just using the API...

I'm personally using the following backdrop filter map (which maps to Tailwind's defaults), and as a suggestion/feedback I'd like to suggest using multiples of **4px** (or just `.25rem`) instead of **5px**.

Something like `4px`, `8px`, `12px`, `16px`, `24px`, `48px` (large blur option is desired, especially for large overlays on big screens).

```
"bd-filter-blur": (
    property: backdrop-filter,
    class: bd-filter-blur,
    values: (
        null: blur(8px),
        none: blur(0),
        sm: blur(4px),
        md: blur(12px),
        lg: blur(16px),
        xl: blur(24px),
        2xl: blur(40px),
        3xl: blur(64px),
    )
),
```
author: pricop

body:
> Your utility doesn't just blur, it also applies a strong saturate() filter, which feels very opinionated (it increases the colors underneath quite a lot, and could hurt accessibility)

Yes, as I mentioned, this is just a rough example to have something to discuss. This needs to be fine-tuned, your example looks better than mine!

> CSS size of Bootstrap v6 currently sits at 275kb~, it has almost doubled from 144kb~

Yes, that's high. Just thinking out loud, components like Card and Alert can be completely built using existing utility classes.
author: crftwrk

body:
I thought of this myself many times... While it's true that some components could be rebuilt using CSS classes, in Bootstrap's case that would be a bad idea. Bootstrap is a design system. Components such as `alert` and `card` are a core part of Bootstrap and they are what it makes it so strong. The moment we turn `
` into `
` we lose the ability to easily customize the components if we change our mind later about our design system later on. Currently if I want to change my UI's spacing to be a bit more loose, all I have to do is to modify a few CSS/SCSS vars, and I'm done, that doesn't work with utility classes. Not only that, but now you'd have to remember what spacing each component is supposed to be, so we'd also lose the ability to easily be consistent. This would be a nightmare when working across multiple projects because now you can't use the component class anymore, but you'd have to either remember the exact CSS properties for the said component instead. And lastly, but not least, semantics... Bootstrap thrives in this area, is very easy to understand by someone that's not familiar with CSS. So someone that's just getting started is going to have a hard time understanding what those classes even are. I wish to see more utility classes added (especially backdrop-filter blur, because I'm making use of this myself too, haha), but I could understand why it might not happening. There are areas where we could remove/reduce some of the code (e.g: for components `display: grid;` we can combine properties such as `align-items: center` and `justify-content: center` with just: `place-items: center;`, same thing with paddings, borders, margins, etc.). I'm curious what's the official take on the CSS size getting bigger.
author: pricop

body:
I totally agree with your reasoning on why prebuilt components are more useful here.

> I'm curious what's the official take on the CSS size getting bigger.

It can only get smaller when removing code which means less functionality. I remember @mdo mentioning concerns about file size when it reached around 200 KB in v5.3 with dark mode.

On the other hand, 275kb CSS size is smaller than a 1920x1280 png from Unsplash, compressed by TinyPNG. Webservers and browsers are incredible fast and functionality needs code/size. 

I see blowing up code everywhere. Most time I work with WordPress and there are (popular) plugins that care about nothing and ships 800kb+ CSS files. Once loaded, these assets are cached anyway.

Given that, I don’t think an increase from ~144 KB in v4 to ~275 KB in v6 is a major issue in 2026 anymore.

Checked the code from your [example](https://github.com/orgs/twbs/discussions/42187#discussioncomment-16176499), this results in tiny 444 bytes minified CSS on top. Does this additional size really matter?
author: crftwrk

body:
444 bytes is not that much. I'd personally love to see `bd-filter-blur` being added.

Wordpress plugins are notoriously known for being very poorly optimized. Just because others are practicing, doesn't mean we should too.

There's a very big distinction to be made here between a 300kb image, and a 300kb CSS size.

Because the browser requires the CSS code to paint the page, a large CSS size can cause layout shifts, FOUC (flash of unstyle content), and even impact Core Vitals, which could potentially hurt SEO performance, while images can just be lazy loaded after the content has been painted.
author: pricop

body:
Regarding file size, one of the more difficult things here is CSS variables. It'll be interesting to review all those and how we generate them moving forward. One little thing I'm doing that we could do more of is the `null` values in our `defaults()`—those get removed automatically by Sass at compile time. There are plenty of default values we could loop over to and fallback to global tokens.

We also need some first class Purge CSS or something. Maybe even a leaner default build could be considered. Few options ahead of us.

body:
Hi @julien-deramond I'm amazed at how much of this has been cleaned up so fast! I'm seeing that even the items not-yet-crossed-out above are mostly already fixed. Amazing.

FYI, a couple things I still see in play:
- For OTP connected inputs, it looks like some of the input groups are still squared off on the right end, while others are appropriately rounded (not immediately clear to me where the discrepancy lies).
- For the Focus Ring, I see the different color rings now but I'm still not seeing it show on click, even though that issue has been closed. I see that the instructions now say "Tab into..." rather than "Click directly on..." so this might be intentional, but this is a change from how focus rings worked in Bootstrap 5 and I'm a little curious why.
author: sgilberg

body:
> * For the Focus Ring, I see the different color rings now but I'm still not seeing it show on click, even though that issue has been closed. I see that the instructions now say "Tab into..." rather than "Click directly on..." so this might be intentional, but this is a change from how focus rings worked in Bootstrap 5 and I'm a little curious why.

This is because we use `:focus-visible` vs `:focus`, where `:focus-visible` is only showing for keyboard users basically.
author: mdo

body: FYI @julien-deramond @mdo for the OTP connected inputs, I figured out why I was still seeing some of the inputs squared off on the right end. It's not inherent to the docs or the styling, but I have a password manager extension which adds a hidden `<span>` element after the last input in the group, making the input that would be the `:last-child` no longer the last child. I'm not sure how often this use case is encountered, or how to mitigate it exactly, but I'm mentioning it in case it helps.

body:
The markup is missing the navbar color mode for a dark/colored background. In Bootstrap 5.3, add `data-bs-theme="dark"` to the `.navbar` when you use a dark background such as `bg-success`: ```html
...
``` `data-bs-target` needs the `#`, but `aria-controls` should be just the id value. Also make sure the Bootstrap JS bundle is loaded if the collapse opens/closes incorrectly; the icon visibility itself is mostly the navbar theme/CSS variables.

author: IArnaut2

body:
Hey Igor,

# This no longer looks like a navbar color issue

The screenshot shows that the toggler button itself exists and has the correct dimensions. Only the three lines are missing.

Bootstrap does not render those lines as HTML. `.navbar-toggler-icon` receives an encoded SVG through the CSS variable `--bs-navbar-toggler-icon-bg`.

Open DevTools, select `.navbar-toggler-icon`, and run:

    const icon = document.querySelector('.navbar-toggler-icon');
    getComputedStyle(icon).backgroundImage;
    getComputedStyle(icon).getPropertyValue('--bs-navbar-toggler-icon-bg');

## Check the result

- If the value is `none` or empty, another stylesheet is overriding Bootstrap's rule or variable.
- If it contains `data:image/svg+xml`, check the browser console for a Content Security Policy error.
- If the Bootstrap CSS request failed, check the Network tab for an SRI, CSP, or CDN error.

A restrictive policy such as `img-src 'self'` will block Bootstrap's embedded `data:` SVG. This exact behavior has previously been reported in Bootstrap:

https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues/39040

The fact that `data-bs-theme="dark"` made no difference makes a blocked or overridden background image especially likely.

# CSP-friendly solution

Instead of weakening an intentional CSP, replace Bootstrap's background image with an inline SVG:

    <button
      class="navbar-toggler text-light"
      type="button"
      data-bs-toggle="collapse"
      data-bs-target="#toggler"
      aria-controls="toggler"
      aria-expanded="false"
      aria-label="Toggle navigation"
    >
      <svg
        width="30"
        height="30"
        viewBox="0 0 30 30"
        fill="none"
        stroke="currentColor"
        stroke-width="2"
        stroke-linecap="round"
        aria-hidden="true"
      >
        <path d="M4 7h22M4 15h22M4 23h22"></path>
      </svg>
    </button>

This uses `currentColor`, so `text-light` makes the lines visible without relying on Bootstrap's encoded background image.

One additional detail: the discussion title says Bootstrap `5.3.8`, but the stylesheet shown in the code is `5.3.7`. That mismatch is probably not the cause, but it is worth testing with the exact CSS and JS bundle from the same version.

Also keep:

- `data-bs-target="#toggler"`
- `aria-controls="toggler"` without `#`
- `aria-label="Toggle navigation"`

The Bootstrap JavaScript bundle controls whether the menu opens. It does not draw the icon itself.

References:

- https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/components/navbar/#toggler
- https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/main/scss/_navbar.scss
- https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues/39040

body:
`.mb-3` is a utility class, and it sets `margin-bottom` to a particular value. You can find these in our spacing or margin/padding docs. These are quite common across various frontend libraries. See https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/utilities/spacing/.

As for things that are specific to HTML as the language, the MDN is your friend here. In v6, we're adding more links to those where appropriate to easily give more context for those learning or generally curious. These are things that are not specific or unique to our project. Here's the input page there: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Reference/Elements/input. Lots of good information.

body:
**Update – v0.1.4 is out! 🚀**

Since my initial post three days ago, I've been heads-down building – and today I'm happy to share that **v0.1.4** just dropped on pub.dev!

This release is a massive step forward. Here's what's new:

---

**🧩 New Components**

- `BsCard` & `BsCardGroup` – Flexible Bootstrap cards with header, footer, image positions (top, bottom, left, right, overlay), color variants, and border variants
- `BsCarousel` & `BsCarouselItem` – Full slideshow support with autoplay, controls, indicators, fade transition, dark mode, touch gestures, and per-slide interval overrides
- `BsDropdown`, `BsDropdownMenu` & `BsDropdownItem` – Highly flexible popup menus with split button support, direction control (up/down/start/end), collision detection & auto-flip, and configurable auto-close behavior
- `BsCollapse` – Smooth vertical and horizontal collapse transitions
- `BsCloseButton` – Dismiss button for alerts, modals, and more
- `BsBreadcrumb` & `BsBreadcrumbItem` – Navigation hierarchy with customizable dividers
- `BsInput` & `BsSelect` – Form controls with native Flutter validation, sizing, focus rings, and `.is-valid` / `.is-invalid` states
- `BsCheckbox` & `BsRange` – Checkboxes, toggle switches, and custom range sliders
- `BsInputGroup` & `BsInputGroupText` – Combined form groups with intelligent border-radius management
- `BsImage` & `BsFigure` – Responsive images, thumbnails, rounded/circular styles, and captioned figures
- `BsTable` – Fully styled tables with striped rows/columns, hover, bordered, borderless, compact, responsive, and contextual variant support
- `BsHeading` – Bootstrap-compatible H1–H6 typography

**🛠️ New Helpers**

- `BsVStack` & `BsHStack` – Vertical/horizontal layout stacks with gap spacing
- `BsRatio` – Aspect ratio utility (16:9, 4:3, 1:1, and more)
- `BsVerticalRule` – Vertical divider
- `BsLink` & `BsIconLink` – Colored links and icon action links

**⚡ New Utility Extensions**

- `.truncate()` on `Text` widgets
- `BsDisplayExtension` – `visible()`, `gone()`, `dNone()`, `opacity()`
- `BsAlignmentExtension` – `center()`, `alignStart()`, `alignEnd()`, `alignTop()`, `alignBottom()`
- `BsSizeExtension` – `w()`, `h()`, `w100()`, `w50()`, `expanded()`, and more

**📚 Documentation**

The full documentation has been completely revamped – every component and utility now has its own dedicated page in both 🇬🇧 English and 🇩🇪 German, including screenshot previews.

---

📦 pub.dev: https://pub.dev/packages/bootstrap_ui_flutter
💻 GitHub: https://github.com/Nexus633/bootstrap_ui_flutter

Feedback, ideas, and contributions are very welcome – the project is fully open source! 🙌
author: Nexus633

body:
**Update: v0.5.0 (Beta) is out – The Massive Component Update! 🚀**

Since my initial post, the library has grown a lot. With v0.5.0, `bootstrap_ui_flutter` officially reaches **Beta status** – and it's the biggest release yet.

**13 new components in one shot:**
`BsModal`, `BsNavbar`, `BsOffcanvas`, `BsToast` + `BsToastManager`, `BsNav` & `BsTab`, `BsTooltip`, `BsPopover`, `BsScrollspy`, `BsListGroup`, `BsPagination`, `BsProgress`, `BsPlaceholder`, `BsSpinner`

**Highlights:**
- 🎯 `BsScrollspy` – automatically highlights the active nav item based on scroll position, just like Bootstrap's JS implementation
- 📬 `BsToastManager` – stackable push notifications in any screen corner, with auto-dismiss
- 📱 `BsNavbar` – fully responsive, with toggler and branding support
- 🎨 Smart contrast: `BsButton` and `BsTooltip` now auto-calculate text color via luminance check
- 💪 Massive extension upgrades for `BsText`, `BsBorder`, and `BsSize` utilities

All component showcases have been redesigned, and full bilingual documentation (EN/DE) is published.

**Already 230+ downloads on pub.dev** – and just getting started.

📦 pub.dev: https://pub.dev/packages/bootstrap_ui_flutter
💻 GitHub: https://github.com/Nexus633/bootstrap_ui_flutter

If you're building Flutter web or desktop apps and want the Bootstrap look & feel – give it a try and let me know what you think! Feedback and feature requests are very welcome. 🙏
author: Nexus633
